title: Aggregations Recognition Track — Mid-Level
description: A 24-exercise mid-level recognition track. Read production-style PySpark aggregation pipelines: window functions, broadcast joins, skew handling, partial aggregation, AQE, time-window semantics, and physical plans. Six modules: JAI1 Windows vs groupBy · JAI2 Broadcast Joins & Skew · JAI3 Partial Aggregation · JAI4 AQE · JAI5 Time Windows · JAI6 Reading Production Code. Companion to PSI1 and PSI3.

datasets.description: Shared NordGrid meter readings (20 rows). Mid-Level scenarios layer 12M-row production scale claims on top of this small sample — the sample verifies correctness, the scale numbers ground the reasoning.
Substation reference table (6 rows). Used in the broadcast-join and refactor-reading scenarios across JAI2, JAI3, JAI4, and JAI6.
Per-customer-type tariffs (4 rows). Used by JAI2.4 ("tariffs grew to 1.8GB" scenario) and JAI6.3 (three-way join plan).

tasks.title: 7-Day Rolling Average — Window Function vs Self-Join

description.context: NordGrid's analytics team computes a 7-day rolling average of daily_kw per region for an operations dashboard. The pipeline runs daily over 12M meter readings. Two engineers submit competing implementations in the same PR. Both produce identical output on the 30-day verification window. Which one should ship to production, and why?
description.task: Both snippets produce identical output on cleanly-spaced data. Which is faster at 12M-row scale, and what is the dominant mechanism?

evidence.title: Goal
evidence.content: For each (region, date), compute the average daily_kw over the trailing 7 calendar days (inclusive of the current date). Output one row per (region, date).

evidence.label: Snippet A — Window function with rowsBetween
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import Window
from pyspark.sql.functions import avg, col

daily = (df
    .groupBy('region', 'date')
    .agg(avg('daily_kw').alias('region_daily_avg')))

w = Window.partitionBy('region').orderBy('date').rowsBetween(-6, 0)

result_a = daily.withColumn('rolling_7d_avg', avg('region_daily_avg').over(w))

evidence.label: Snippet B — Self-join on date range
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import avg, col, datediff, expr

daily = (df
    .groupBy('region', 'date')
    .agg(avg('daily_kw').alias('region_daily_avg')))

left = daily.alias('L')
right = daily.alias('R')

result_b = (left.join(right,
        (col('L.region') == col('R.region')) &
        (datediff(col('L.date'), col('R.date')).between(0, 6)))
    .groupBy(col('L.region').alias('region'), col('L.date').alias('date'),
             col('L.region_daily_avg').alias('region_daily_avg'))
    .agg(avg(col('R.region_daily_avg')).alias('rolling_7d_avg')))

fields.label: Both snippets produce identical output on cleanly-spaced data. Which is faster at 12M-row scale, and what is the dominant mechanism?
options: Snippet B is faster
Snippet A is faster
They are equivalent in cost
Snippet A is faster only when the date range fits in memory; for any window longer than 7
fields.correctAnswer: Snippet A is faster
fields.rationale:
Snippet A uses Window.partitionBy('region').orderBy('date').rowsBetween(-6, 0). The plan triggers exactly one shuffle (Exchange hashpartitioning(region)), one sort within each partition (Sort), and a single window-pass that streams the 7-row trailing aggregate. Total shuffle volume: one full pass of the daily DataFrame.

Snippet B's range-join is a Cartesian-style explosion. For each row in left, the join condition matches up to 7 rows in right (the trailing 7 days). The intermediate result is roughly 7× the size of daily before the second groupBy reduces it back. Spark cannot use a hash join for a range condition (datediff between 0 and 6 is not an equality), so it falls back to either a SortMergeJoin with the range as a residual filter or a BroadcastNestedLoopJoin on the right side — both expensive. Then the groupBy triggers a second shuffle. Total work: one sort, one expanded join, one second shuffle, one second aggregation.

At 12M rows aggregated to ~120 (region, date) cells the difference is small in absolute terms — but the principle generalizes. At per-meter granularity (where the same idiom is needed for per-meter rolling averages), the row count is 12M and the 7× expansion materializes 84M intermediate rows. Window functions are the canonical tool for trailing aggregates precisely because they avoid this explosion.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Inverts the parallelism story. The window function in Snippet A parallelizes naturally across partitions of region — each region's window runs on its own executor independently. The self-join in Snippet B parallelizes too, but on a much larger intermediate dataset.

Option C: Overestimates Catalyst's pattern-recognition. Catalyst does not detect the rolling-window pattern in a range join and rewrite it as a window function. The two plans remain different — the engineer must choose the right idiom.

Option D: Confuses rolling-window cost with window length. Both approaches pay a cost proportional to window length, but Snippet A pays it once (in the trailing-aggregate stream) while Snippet B pays it as a row-expansion that then has to be reduced again.

tasks.psiReference: PSI1 lesson 4 (Window Functions in the Plan); PSI3 lesson 6 (Range Joins and Their Cost)

tasks.title: Top Meter Per Region: groupBy+join Loses Ties

description.context: An engineer is reading a snippet that produces a leaderboard of the top meter per region by daily_kw. They want to predict the row count of the output before running it. The 12M-row meter table has 4 regions. Within NORTH, two meters happen to record exactly the same maximum daily_kw on the same date — a known edge case the operations team has flagged.
description.task: Which line contains the bug, and what is the underlying mechanism?

evidence.label: snippet.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, max as spark_max

# Goal: the leaderboard expects exactly one row per region.

maxes = (df
    .groupBy('region')
    .agg(spark_max('daily_kw').alias('max_kw')))

top_per_region = df.join(
    maxes,
    (df['region'] == maxes['region']) & (df['daily_kw'] == maxes['max_kw']),
    how='inner'
).select(df['region'], df['meter_id'], df['daily_kw'])

top_per_region.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('gold/leaderboard/')

evidence.title: Observed behavior
evidence.content: The job runs successfully on the 12M-row production dataset. The downstream leaderboard consumer reports an inconsistency: on most days the leaderboard has 4 rows (one per region) as expected, but roughly once per week the leaderboard has 5 rows. The fifth row is always in NORTH or SOUTH. The engineer reviewing the output cannot reproduce it on their dev sample of 50K rows.

fields.label: Which line contains the bug, and what is the underlying mechanism?
options: Line 9: the groupBy('region') should be groupBy('region', 'date')
Line 13: the join key (df['region'] == maxes['region']) is wrong
Line 13: the join condition matches every meter that ties for the maximum
Line 17: write.mode('overwrite') is racing with a concurrent reader
fields.correctAnswer: Line 13: the join condition matches every meter that ties for the maximum
fields.rationale:
The bug is the classic groupBy-then-join-back tie ambiguity. groupBy('region').agg(max('daily_kw')) produces one row per region with the max value. The join then matches every meter row whose (region, daily_kw) equals (region, max_kw). When two meters tie for the maximum, the equi-join returns both — and the leaderboard gets two rows for that region. The output count therefore equals 4 + (number of ties), not 4.

The dev sample of 50K rows did not surface the bug because random ties at exactly the max are rare on small data. At 12M rows with values rounded to one decimal place, ties surface several times per week.

The canonical mid-level fix is row_number() over a window:

  w = Window.partitionBy('region').orderBy(col('daily_kw').desc(), col('meter_id'))
  top = df.withColumn('rn', row_number().over(w)).filter(col('rn') == 1).drop('rn')

row_number() guarantees a strict 1..N ordering by including a tie-break (meter_id), so exactly one row survives per region. rank() and dense_rank() do not solve the bug — they assign equal values to ties, so two tied meters both get rank 1.

This bug is the canonical pedagogical case for window-vs-groupBy: groupBy + join is a perfectly correct idiom when the aggregate value is unique, and a silent correctness bug when it is not. The reading habit to internalize: any groupBy-then-rejoin pattern on the aggregate value should make the engineer ask 'can this value tie?' before merging.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Misreads the business goal. The leaderboard is the all-time maximum per region, not a per-day report. groupBy granularity is a possible design choice but not the bug.

Option B: Confuses syntax with semantics. Both equality predicates and on= produce the same join semantics. The duplicate-region-column issue is real but is not the cause of the extra rows.

Option D: Looks for a write-side concurrency bug. write.mode('overwrite') replaces the entire path atomically — it does not produce extra rows. The extra row is in top_per_region's logical plan, not in the file write.

tasks.psiReference: PSI1 lesson 4 (Window Functions in the Plan); PSI3 lesson 2 (Join Strategies and When Each Is Cheaper)

tasks.title: Running Total: rangeBetween Without orderBy

description.context: An engineer is reading a snippet that computes the cumulative monthly billed kWh per substation across the year. The output feeds a regulatory report. They want to predict what the snippet returns for substation S-N-02 (Hamburg-Süd) for a hypothetical month where two readings exist: 100 kWh on day 5 and 150 kWh on day 20. There are no other readings for that substation that month.
description.task: For substation S-N-02 in the hypothetical month with readings of 100 (day 5) and 150 (day 20), what does the `mtd_total` column hold on each of the two output rows?

evidence.label: snippet.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import Window
from pyspark.sql.functions import sum, col, month, year

monthly = (df
    .withColumn('y', year('date'))
    .withColumn('m', month('date'))
    .groupBy('substation_id', 'y', 'm', 'date')
    .agg(sum('daily_kw').alias('day_total')))

w = Window.partitionBy('substation_id', 'y', 'm')

result = monthly.withColumn('mtd_total', sum('day_total').over(w))

result.filter(col('substation_id') == 'S-N-02').show()

fields.label: For substation S-N-02 in the hypothetical month with readings of 100 (day 5) and 150 (day 20), what does the `mtd_total` column hold on each of the two output rows?
options: Row for day 5: mtd_total = 100.
Row for day 5: mtd_total = 250.
Row for day 5: mtd_total = null.
An AnalysisException is raised at the .over(w) call because Window.partitionBy() without
fields.correctAnswer: Row for day 5: mtd_total = 250.
fields.rationale:
The window specification Window.partitionBy('substation_id', 'y', 'm') has no orderBy and no rowsBetween/rangeBetween. By PySpark's default rules, when no orderBy is specified, the frame defaults to the entire partition — i.e., unboundedPreceding to unboundedFollowing. This means sum('day_total').over(w) computes the total for the entire partition (the whole month for that substation) and assigns the same value to every row in the partition.

For S-N-02 in the hypothetical month, the partition contains both rows. mtd_total = 100 + 150 = 250 on both. There is no 'cumulative' behavior because cumulative semantics require orderBy + rowsBetween(unboundedPreceding, currentRow).

The engineer who wrote 'mtd_total' in the column name almost certainly intended cumulative-month-to-date, which would require:

  w = Window.partitionBy('substation_id', 'y', 'm').orderBy('date').rowsBetween(Window.unboundedPreceding, Window.currentRow)

The absence of orderBy is the canonical mid-level mistake: the column name documents the intent, but the window does something else. PySpark does not raise an error because the operation is technically valid — sum over an unordered partition is well-defined; it is just not what the engineer wanted.

Option D is the trap for engineers who remember that some functions (row_number, rank, lag, lead) require orderBy. Aggregate functions like sum and avg do not — they accept Window.partitionBy() alone and apply over the full partition. This asymmetry is the technical detail behind the bug.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Reads the column name 'mtd_total' as documentation of behavior. Cumulative semantics require an explicit orderBy + frame; the column name has no effect on the plan.

Option C: Imagines window-frame boundary semantics that do not exist. PySpark windows do not exclude their first row by default; with no ordering, every row is in the same frame.

Option D: Overgeneralizes the orderBy requirement. row_number, rank, lag, lead require orderBy. Aggregate functions (sum, avg, min, max, count) do not — they apply over the whole partition by default. The bug runs without complaint.

tasks.psiReference: PSI1 lesson 4 (Window Functions in the Plan); PSI1 lesson 5 (Frame Semantics: rowsBetween and rangeBetween)

tasks.title: Top 3 Per Region: row_number vs percentile_approx Threshold

description.context: An engineer needs to produce 'the top 3 highest-consumption meters per region' for an investigation report on suspected industrial overconsumption. Two implementations are on the table. Both run on the 12M-row meter dataset and both produce roughly 12 rows of output (3 per region across 4 regions). Which one solves the stated problem correctly, and what does the other one actually compute?
description.task: Which snippet correctly produces exactly the top 3 per region, and what does the other one actually compute?

evidence.title: Goal
evidence.content: For each region, return the 3 highest-daily_kw meter readings — exactly 3 per region.

evidence.label: Snippet A — row_number with a Window
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import Window
from pyspark.sql.functions import row_number, col

w = Window.partitionBy('region').orderBy(col('daily_kw').desc(), col('meter_id'))

top3 = (df
    .filter(col('daily_kw').isNotNull() & col('region').isNotNull())
    .withColumn('rn', row_number().over(w))
    .filter(col('rn') <= 3)
    .drop('rn'))

evidence.label: Snippet B — percentile_approx threshold then filter
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import percentile_approx, col

thresholds = (df
    .filter(col('daily_kw').isNotNull() & col('region').isNotNull())
    .groupBy('region')
    .agg(percentile_approx('daily_kw', 0.99975).alias('threshold')))

top3 = (df.join(thresholds, on='region')
    .filter(col('daily_kw') >= col('threshold')))

fields.label: Which snippet correctly produces exactly the top 3 per region, and what does the other one actually compute?
options: Both produce identical output
Snippet A is correct.
Snippet B is correct because percentile_approx is the canonical Spark idiom for top-N.
Neither is correct
fields.correctAnswer: Snippet A is correct.
fields.rationale:
Snippet A is the canonical mid-level idiom for exact top-N per group:
  Window.partitionBy('region').orderBy(daily_kw desc, meter_id) → row_number() → filter rn ≤ 3.
The orderBy includes meter_id as a tie-break, so row_number assigns a strict 1..N order even when daily_kw values tie. Filtering rn ≤ 3 returns exactly 3 rows per region, every time.

Snippet B is a fundamentally different operation. percentile_approx('daily_kw', 0.99975) returns an approximate threshold value — the daily_kw level above which roughly 0.025% of rows fall. The engineer's reasoning (12M / 4 regions = 3M per region; 3/3M = 0.0001 ≈ 0.99999 quantile, fudged to 0.99975) is unsound for three reasons:

1. Quantiles are about value distribution, not rank. If 50 meters all have the same near-maximum value, all 50 cross the threshold and survive the filter. The output then has 50 rows for that region, not 3.
2. percentile_approx is approximate — its accuracy parameter defaults to 10000, meaning the returned value can be slightly off the true quantile. The threshold may include or exclude rows that the exact top-3 would not.
3. The quantile assumes uniform distribution per region. NORTH might have 5M meters, WEST might have 1M. The same quantile produces different absolute thresholds and different row counts per region.

The pedagogical point: percentile-based filtering is not a substitute for top-N. They solve different problems. percentile_approx answers 'what is the 99.975th percentile value?' — a useful metric for distribution-shape questions. row_number with a Window answers 'which 3 rows are largest?' — the precise top-N question.

A more subtle reason mid-level engineers reach for Snippet B: they remember that percentile_approx benefits from partial aggregation (Spark has an internal mergeable accumulator for it) and is therefore fast at scale. That is true but irrelevant — the speed advantage means nothing if the operation does not solve the right problem.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Confuses 'approximately the top 3' with 'exactly the top 3'. Quantile-based thresholds bound the value, not the row count.

Option C: Inverts the determinism story. row_number is deterministic when the orderBy is total (here the meter_id tie-break makes it total). percentile_approx is the non-deterministic one — its accuracy parameter introduces approximation.

Option D: Misapplies the orderBy + limit pattern. orderBy + limit gives the global top-N, not top-N per group. Per-group top-N requires either a Window function or a self-join + groupBy + count, and the Window approach is canonical.

tasks.psiReference: PSI1 lesson 4 (Window Functions in the Plan); PSI3 lesson 8 (percentile_approx and Approximate Aggregates)

tasks.title: Aggregate Then Join, or Join Then Aggregate

description.context: An engineer is reviewing two implementations of a 'capacity utilization per substation' report. The pipeline must compute total daily_kw per substation and divide by the substation's capacity_mw. The 12M-row meters DataFrame must combine with the 120-row substations reference table. Both implementations produce identical output. The engineer is asked to recommend which one ships.
description.task: Both produce identical output. Which is cheaper at 12M-row scale, and what is the dominant mechanism?

evidence.title: Goal
evidence.content: Compute total daily_kw per substation_id and produce one row per substation enriched with the substation's name, region, and capacity_mw.

evidence.label: Snippet A — Join 12M-row meters to substations first, then aggregate
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import sum, col

result_a = (meters_12m
    .join(substations, on='substation_id', how='inner')
    .groupBy('substation_id', 'name', 'region', 'capacity_mw')
    .agg(sum('daily_kw').alias('total_kw')))

evidence.label: Snippet B — Aggregate to per-substation rows first, then join
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import sum, col

per_sub = (meters_12m
    .groupBy('substation_id')
    .agg(sum('daily_kw').alias('total_kw')))

result_b = per_sub.join(substations, on='substation_id', how='inner')

fields.label: Both produce identical output. Which is cheaper at 12M-row scale, and what is the dominant mechanism?
options: They cost the same
Snippet A is cheaper
Snippet B is cheaper
Snippet A is cheaper because joins use BroadcastHashJoin at this scale (substations is
fields.correctAnswer: Snippet B is cheaper
fields.rationale:
The principle is 'aggregation is a row-reducer; reduce before you enrich'. Snippet B reduces 12M rows to roughly 120 (one per substation_id) before doing anything else with them. The join afterwards is between a 120-row aggregate and a 120-row reference — Catalyst will broadcast either side and the join is effectively free.

Snippet A pushes all 12M meter rows through the join first. Even with a broadcast join (substations is well under autoBroadcastJoinThreshold), the broadcast side is broadcast to every executor and every meter row is enriched. The wide intermediate DataFrame (12M rows × ~10 columns) is then shuffled by groupBy. The shuffle volume is roughly 12M × column_width, vs Snippet B's ~120 × column_width post-aggregation. The difference is two to three orders of magnitude.

The Catalyst optimizer does not automatically rewrite Snippet A into Snippet B (option A). Logically the rewrite is sound only if no join column is an aggregate input — in this case sum('daily_kw') doesn't depend on substations columns, but Catalyst's rule library does not include this aggregate-pushdown rule for joins. The engineer must apply the principle by hand.

The broader principle (the one mid-level engineers internalize through this exercise): when a join produces a row for every input on one side, and a downstream aggregation reduces that to a few rows per group, ask 'can I aggregate first?' before writing the join. The answer is yes when the aggregation columns and the join keys are independent, which is true in most enrichment scenarios — and that is most of what production ETL does.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Overestimates Catalyst's structural rewrites. Catalyst optimizes within plans (predicate pushdown, projection pruning, join reordering for cost) but does not push aggregations down through joins as a structural rewrite. The engineer chooses the order.

Option B: Misreads the partition reuse. The join's hash partitioning is on substation_id, which happens to match the groupBy's partitioning — so technically Spark can reuse the shuffle. But this is a marginal optimization that does not offset the cost of pushing 12M rows through the join in the first place.

Option D: Counts shuffles correctly but misweights their cost. Both snippets trigger shuffles. Snippet A's groupBy shuffle moves 12M rows; Snippet B's groupBy shuffle moves 12M rows too, but the post-shuffle aggregation produces only ~120 rows that need no further shuffle for the small join. The economic ratio is 12M/120, not 1/2.

tasks.psiReference: PSI3 lesson 1 (Why Pushing Aggregations Past Joins Pays); PSI3 lesson 3 (Broadcast Join Mechanics)

tasks.title: Reading a Broadcast Join Plan

description.context: An engineer is debugging a pipeline that joins meter readings to the substations dimension and aggregates by region. They run df_result.explain(mode='formatted') and see the plan below. The substations table has 120 rows (~12 KB). The meters DataFrame has 12M rows (~1.2 GB). spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold is at its default (10MB). The engineer wrote no .hint('broadcast') in the code.
description.task: Reading the plan above, what is the correct interpretation?

evidence.label: df.explain() — physical plan
evidence.content:
== Physical Plan ==
AdaptiveSparkPlan isFinalPlan=false
+- HashAggregate(keys=[region#42], functions=[sum(daily_kw#39)])
   +- Exchange hashpartitioning(region#42, 200), ENSURE_REQUIREMENTS, [plan_id=87]
      +- HashAggregate(keys=[region#42], functions=[partial_sum(daily_kw#39)])
         +- Project [daily_kw#39, region#42]
            +- BroadcastHashJoin [substation_id#41], [substation_id#15], Inner, BuildRight
               :- Filter isnotnull(substation_id#41)
               :  +- FileScan parquet bronze.meters[meter_id#37,daily_kw#39,date#40,substation_id#41,region#42]
               +- BroadcastExchange HashedRelationBroadcastMode(List(input[0, string, true]))
                  +- Filter isnotnull(substation_id#15)
                     +- FileScan parquet silver.substations[substation_id#15,name#16,region_dim#17,capacity_mw#18]

fields.label: Reading the plan above, what is the correct interpretation?
options: The plan triggers two shuffles: one for the join (BroadcastHashJoin requires shuffling
The plan triggers exactly one shuffle (the Exchange above the partial HashAggregate).
The plan triggers two shuffles: one BroadcastExchange and one Exchange hashpartitioning.
AQE switched the strategy at runtime
fields.correctAnswer: The plan triggers exactly one shuffle (the Exchange above the partial HashAggregate).
fields.rationale:
Two operators look like exchanges in this plan, but only one is a shuffle:

1. **BroadcastExchange** (under the BroadcastHashJoin's right side): this is a broadcast, not a shuffle. The 120-row substations dimension is collected to the driver, packaged as a HashedRelation, and sent once to every executor. No data movement between executors — every executor has the full dimension. Cost is roughly the dimension size × executor count, paid once.

2. **Exchange hashpartitioning(region, 200)**: this is the actual shuffle. After the join enriches each meter row with its region from the dimension, partial_sum is computed locally per partition (the lower HashAggregate with 'partial_sum'). The Exchange then shuffles the partial sums by region — only ~4 distinct regions × ~200 input partitions = up to 800 small partial-result rows are shuffled, vastly less than the 12M input rows.

The presence of partial_sum in the lower HashAggregate and sum in the upper HashAggregate is the partial+final aggregation pattern (more on this in JAI3). The combiner reduces the shuffle volume by a factor of ~50,000× (12M rows down to ~800 partial rows), which is why partial aggregation matters so much at scale.

The plan also confirms two things by their absence:
- No broadcast() hint was used. The strategy is BroadcastHashJoin BuildRight because Catalyst's CBO measured silver.substations and saw it was under the autoBroadcastJoinThreshold.
- AQE's runtime interventions (AQEShuffleRead, CustomShuffleReader, skewJoin=true) are not yet visible — the plan is pre-execution (isFinalPlan=false). After execution, AQE may coalesce the post-shuffle partitions if the Exchange output is small. That intervention would not show in this static plan.

The pedagogical point: BroadcastExchange and Exchange look similar in a formatted plan, and both contain the word 'Exchange', but their physical meanings are different. Reading mid-level plans correctly requires distinguishing them.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Inverts the broadcast-join cost model. BroadcastHashJoin specifically does not require shuffling either side — it broadcasts one side to skip the shuffle on the other. That is the mechanism's whole point.

Option C: Counts every Exchange-named operator as a shuffle. BroadcastExchange is a separate operator class — it broadcasts via the driver, not via inter-executor shuffle. The plan's shuffle count is the count of Exchange (not BroadcastExchange) nodes.

Option D: Misreads AdaptiveSparkPlan. AdaptiveSparkPlan with isFinalPlan=false means AQE has not yet observed runtime statistics for this plan — the join strategy and shuffle count shown are the post-Catalyst, pre-AQE plan. AQE adjusts the plan after stage boundaries; it does not change the join strategy in flight on a sub-stage.

tasks.psiReference: PSI1 lesson 6 (BroadcastHashJoin and BroadcastExchange); PSI3 lesson 3 (Broadcast Join Mechanics)

tasks.title: Skew in the Plan: Uneven Task Shuffle Read

description.context: An engineer is debugging a 'slow groupBy by customer_type' on the 12M-row meters table. The pipeline groupBys by customer_type and computes total daily_kw. Customer-type distribution at NordGrid is RESIDENTIAL 60%, COMMERCIAL 25%, INDUSTRIAL 12%, PROSUMER 3%. The job runs but the last stage drags. The engineer pulls the Spark UI's stage detail and sees the per-task shuffle-read distribution below. AQE is enabled with default settings.
description.task: What does this plan and per-task distribution most likely indicate?

evidence.label: df.explain() — physical plan
evidence.content:
Stage 4 — final HashAggregate, 200 tasks

Per-task shuffle-read size (sorted descending):
  Task 137:    7.2 GB    (1 task)
  Task 41:     3.0 GB    (1 task)
  Task 88:     1.4 GB    (1 task)
  Task  2:     0.4 GB    (1 task)
  Tasks 0..199 (other):  ~50 MB each (196 tasks)

Stage 4 elapsed time: 18 min  (previous stage took 90 sec)
Longest single task: Task 137 — 14 min

== Plan excerpt for Stage 4 ==
HashAggregate(keys=[customer_type#43], functions=[sum(daily_kw#39)])
+- Exchange hashpartitioning(customer_type#43, 200), ENSURE_REQUIREMENTS, [plan_id=104]
   +- HashAggregate(keys=[customer_type#43], functions=[partial_sum(daily_kw#39)])

fields.label: What does this plan and per-task distribution most likely indicate?
options: Random shuffle imbalance
Customer-type skew
Disk corruption on the executor running Task 137.
AQE coalesce engaged successfully
fields.correctAnswer: Customer-type skew
fields.rationale:
The fingerprint is unmistakable when you know what to look for. groupBy by a low-cardinality, skewed key (customer_type with 4 distinct values, RESIDENTIAL 60%) hashes every row of the same key to the same partition. Out of 200 partitions configured by spark.sql.shuffle.partitions, only 4 receive any data — the other 196 are empty (the ~50 MB shown is rounding noise from operator state, not actual data). The 4 non-empty partitions hold:

  RESIDENTIAL: 60% of 12 GB ≈ 7.2 GB → Task 137
  COMMERCIAL:  25% of 12 GB ≈ 3.0 GB → Task 41
  INDUSTRIAL:  12% of 12 GB ≈ 1.4 GB → Task 88
  PROSUMER:     3% of 12 GB ≈ 0.4 GB → Task 2

This is the textbook key-cardinality skew pattern. AQE's skew-join handling does not engage here for a specific reason: AQE's skew handling targets *within-key* skew in joins (one key on the build side has many more matching rows than other keys), where it salts the skewed key by splitting it across multiple partitions. AQE does not split a single groupBy key's rows across partitions because doing so would break the aggregation semantics — every row for the same key must land on the same partition for the final HashAggregate to produce one row per key.

The canonical mid-level fix is **two-stage aggregation with salting**:

  from pyspark.sql.functions import lit, rand, floor

  salted = df.withColumn('salt', floor(rand() * 16))
  partial = (salted.groupBy('customer_type', 'salt')
      .agg(sum('daily_kw').alias('partial_sum')))
  final = (partial.groupBy('customer_type')
      .agg(sum('partial_sum').alias('total')))

The salt column splits each customer_type into 16 sub-keys for the first stage, so RESIDENTIAL becomes 16 partitions × ~450 MB each instead of 1 partition × 7.2 GB. The second stage merges the 16 sub-aggregates per customer_type — cheap because it operates on 4 × 16 = 64 small rows.

Option D (AQE coalesce success) is the seductive distractor. AQE coalesce *would* produce few large partitions if the input had been shuffle-balanced — but coalescing *empty* partitions does nothing useful. The 196 small partitions are not a coalesce success; they are a starvation indicator.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Attributes the imbalance to randomness. The 60-25-12-3 percentages of the four bins exactly match the customer-type distribution — this is structural, not stochastic. Re-running produces the same shape.

Option C: Reaches for a hardware/storage explanation when the data-distribution explanation is right there in the percentages. Disk corruption produces task failures and retries, not a single fast-completing 14-min task.

Option D: Mistakes empty partitions for coalesced ones. AQE coalesce reports its work via AQEShuffleRead coalesced='true' in the post-execution plan; it does not silently produce 196 empty partitions. The four large partitions are the unsalted, unbroken keys themselves.

tasks.psiReference: PSI3 lesson 4 (Recognizing Skew from Spark UI Metrics); PSI3 lesson 5 (Salting and Two-Stage Aggregation)

tasks.title: The Broadcast Hint That OOMed the Driver

description.context: Six months ago an engineer added .hint('broadcast') to the tariffs join in NordGrid's billing pipeline because Spark UI showed it was using SortMergeJoin and the join took 4 minutes. The hint dropped the join time to 30 seconds — a clear win. Today, the same pipeline failed in production with 'java.lang.OutOfMemoryError: GC overhead limit exceeded' on the driver. The engineer is asked to find the bug. Six months ago the tariffs table had 4 rows. Today it has grown.
description.task: Which line contains the bug, and what is the underlying mechanism?

evidence.label: snippet.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import sum, col, broadcast

spark.conf.set('spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold', 10 * 1024 * 1024)

# Read the tariffs reference table
tariffs = spark.read.parquet('silver/tariffs/')

# Read 12M-row meter data
meters = spark.read.parquet('bronze/meters/2026-04-*/')

# Join meters to tariffs and compute monthly billing
billing = (meters
    .join(broadcast(tariffs), on='customer_type', how='inner')
    .withColumn('eur_billed', col('daily_kw') * col('eur_per_kw'))
    .groupBy('customer_type', 'region')
    .agg(sum('eur_billed').alias('total_eur')))

billing.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('gold/monthly_billing/')

evidence.title: Observed behavior
evidence.content: The job fails with 'java.lang.OutOfMemoryError: GC overhead limit exceeded' on the driver process — not on an executor. Failure happens before any tasks start. Six months ago this exact code worked. The tariffs Parquet file on disk is now 1.8 GB (compressed); when uncompressed and held as a HashedRelation it would be larger. The engineer confirms autoBroadcastJoinThreshold is 10 MB.

fields.label: Which line contains the bug, and what is the underlying mechanism?
options: Line 13: broadcast(tariffs) forces the build side to be collected to the driver before
Line 3: spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold is set too low for production data.
Line 11: meters at 12M rows is too large to broadcast.
Line 19: the partitioning of the write step is missing. groupBy('customer_type', 'region')
fields.correctAnswer: Line 13: broadcast(tariffs) forces the build side to be collected to the driver before
fields.rationale:
broadcast(tariffs) is an explicit instruction to Spark: 'use BroadcastHashJoin with tariffs as the build side, regardless of size'. The mechanics:

1. The driver runs a collect() on the tariffs DataFrame to materialize it into driver memory.
2. The driver wraps the collected rows into a HashedRelation (a hash-keyed in-memory data structure).
3. The driver broadcasts the HashedRelation to every executor.

Step 1 is the failure point. The 1.8 GB compressed Parquet expands when read into memory (decompressed Parquet plus Java object overhead is typically 3–5× the on-disk size, so 6–10 GB held in driver memory). The default driver memory is 1–2 GB. The driver OOMs before broadcast even begins.

The `.hint('broadcast')` and the explicit `broadcast()` function override autoBroadcastJoinThreshold completely. Catalyst does not check the size when an explicit hint is given — it treats the hint as the engineer's commitment that the side fits. That commitment was true six months ago when tariffs had 4 rows; it became false silently as PROSUMER tariff variants were added (per-region-per-time-of-day rates blew up the row count).

The fix has two parts:

1. **Immediate fix**: remove the broadcast() hint. Catalyst will then check tariffs against autoBroadcastJoinThreshold (10 MB), see it exceeds, and pick SortMergeJoin. The pipeline runs slower than the broadcast version did six months ago — but it runs.

2. **Root-cause fix**: ask why tariffs grew to 1.8 GB. If the per-region-per-time-of-day variants are needed, the join is correctly SortMergeJoin at this scale — accept the cost. If not, prune the tariffs table (filter to currently-active tariffs before the join) so the broadcast hint becomes safe again.

The pedagogical point: broadcast hints are not guarantees of correctness or safety — they are unconditional overrides. The autoBroadcastJoinThreshold mechanism exists precisely to protect against this failure mode. Engineers who add hints are signing up to monitor the side's size forever. The reading-discipline rule: a `.hint('broadcast')` in a code review needs an accompanying assertion that the side stays small, or the hint is a future incident.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option B: Treats autoBroadcastJoinThreshold as a ceiling-with-warnings. It is not — it is the threshold below which Catalyst broadcasts automatically. Raising it to 2 GB would in fact make the OOM worse (Catalyst would broadcast tariffs without the engineer asking, on every job that joins to it). The broadcast() hint is what bypasses the threshold check; raising the threshold is the wrong direction.

Option C: Inverts the broadcast() function's argument semantics. broadcast(tariffs) explicitly marks tariffs as the build side. PySpark does not silently swap the build side. The argument to broadcast() is the side that gets collected to the driver.

Option D: Reaches for a write-step explanation that does not match the failure mode. The driver OOM happens before any tasks start (the engineer specified this in the observation) — the write step is not yet on the critical path when failure occurs.

tasks.psiReference: PSI3 lesson 3 (Broadcast Join Mechanics); PSI3 lesson 9 (When Broadcast Hints Become Liabilities)

tasks.title: collect_list at Scale

description.context: An engineer is reading a snippet from an audit-trail pipeline that collects every meter_id observed per region. The pipeline runs on the 12M-row meters table; the NORTH region accounts for roughly 3M of those rows. They want to predict what the `all_meters` column actually contains for NORTH after this aggregation runs.
description.task: What does the `all_meters` column contain for the NORTH region after this code runs?

evidence.label: snippet.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import collect_list, col

# Goal: produce an audit list of every meter_id seen per region for the month.
region_meters = (df
    .filter(col('region').isNotNull())
    .groupBy('region')
    .agg(collect_list('meter_id').alias('all_meters')))

fields.label: What does the `all_meters` column contain for the NORTH region after this code runs?
options: An array of ~3M meter_ids in the order they appeared in the original CSV file (input order
An array of ~3M meter_ids in lexicographic order
An array of ~3M meter_ids in undefined order
An array of unique meter_ids (deduplicated)
fields.correctAnswer: An array of ~3M meter_ids in undefined order
fields.rationale:
collect_list aggregates values by appending them to a buffer. Within a single partition, the buffer is built in row-arrival order. But the final array per group is the concatenation of partial buffers from many partitions (after the partial+final aggregation pattern), and the order in which those partial buffers are merged depends on partition arrival order at the final HashAggregate — which is non-deterministic across runs.

For NORTH with ~3M rows, the input is split across many input partitions (depending on file layout and shuffle.partitions). Each partition's partial collect_list produces a sub-array in its own arrival order. The final stage concatenates them; the concatenation order is whatever order the post-shuffle partition reads return. Two runs of the same job produce arrays with the same elements but different orderings.

Three side concerns the snippet hides:

1. **Memory pressure**: a 3M-element array in a single column value is a problem. Spark holds the entire array in memory during the aggregation, and downstream operations (write, further transformations) carry the array around. Most production audit-trail pipelines instead emit one row per (region, meter_id) and aggregate downstream.

2. **Partial-aggregation behavior**: collect_list does have a mergeable buffer (concatenate two arrays), so it does benefit from partial aggregation. But the partial benefit is small because the array does not shrink — concatenation just reorganizes the same data. Compare to sum, where partial sums collapse 3M rows to a few partial-sum integers per partition.

3. **collect_set vs collect_list**: collect_set deduplicates. If the engineer wants unique meter_ids per region, collect_set is the right operator. collect_list keeps duplicates — and at 3M rows with most meters reading multiple days, the array contains heavy duplication.

The pedagogical point: aggregate functions are not all created equal. Functions whose output size is bounded by the cardinality of the group key (sum, count, max, avg) are well-behaved at scale. Functions whose output size is proportional to the row count of the group (collect_list, collect_set on high-cardinality input) are scaling traps.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Confuses 'preserves duplicates' with 'preserves order'. collect_list keeps every value; the order in which it presents them is not part of the contract. Engineers who learned aggregation through pandas DataFrames sometimes carry the wrong expectation here — pandas' analogous operation often does preserve a deterministic order on small data.

Option B: Imagines that Spark sorts collect_list output for determinism. It does not — sorting would defeat the partial-aggregation benefit (you cannot mergesort partial buffers without reorganizing the entire collection). Determinism is achieved by adding sort_array(...) afterwards if needed.

Option D: Mistakes collect_list for collect_set. collect_list keeps duplicates; collect_set removes them. The function name reflects the semantic — list semantics keep duplicates, set semantics deduplicate.

tasks.psiReference: PSI1 lesson 7 (Partial Aggregation: When It Helps and When It Does Not); PSI1 lesson 9 (Aggregate Functions and Mergeable Buffers)

tasks.title: Partial Then Final Aggregation

description.context: An engineer runs df.groupBy('region').agg(sum('daily_kw')).explain() and sees the plan excerpt below. They want to understand what each HashAggregate stage is doing and why there are two of them.
description.task: What is the role of each HashAggregate stage in this plan?

evidence.label: snippet.py
evidence.content:
== Physical Plan ==
AdaptiveSparkPlan isFinalPlan=false
+- HashAggregate(keys=[region#42], functions=[sum(daily_kw#39)])
   +- Exchange hashpartitioning(region#42, 200), ENSURE_REQUIREMENTS
      +- HashAggregate(keys=[region#42], functions=[partial_sum(daily_kw#39)])
         +- FileScan parquet bronze.meters[daily_kw#39, region#42]

fields.label: What is the role of each HashAggregate stage in this plan?
options: The lower HashAggregate (partial_sum) is a redundant duplicate that the engineer's old
The lower HashAggregate runs after the shuffle to merge already-aggregated values.
The lower HashAggregate aggregates within each input partition before the shuffle (the
AQE injects the partial stage at runtime when it detects that the input is large enough.
fields.correctAnswer: The lower HashAggregate aggregates within each input partition before the shuffle (the
fields.rationale:
The two-stage HashAggregate pattern is the **partial + final** aggregation that Catalyst applies whenever the aggregate function has a mergeable buffer. Reading bottom-to-top (the order operators execute):

1. **FileScan** reads bronze.meters split into many input partitions (typically one per file or one per ~128 MB block).

2. **HashAggregate (partial_sum)** runs *within each input partition*. It groups the rows in that partition by region and computes a per-partition sum. If a partition holds 100K rows across 4 regions, it emits 4 rows — one per region — each with the partial sum from that partition. Total rows across all input partitions: ~ (input_partitions × 4) instead of the original 12M.

3. **Exchange hashpartitioning(region, 200)** shuffles those small partial rows by region. The shuffle moves only the partial-aggregated rows — orders of magnitude less data than would cross the network without the partial stage.

4. **HashAggregate (sum)** runs *across all post-shuffle partitions*. It groups by region (now collocated by the shuffle) and merges the partial sums into final per-region totals. Output: one row per region.

This 'combiner' pattern is the single largest performance reason groupBy works at scale. Without it, the shuffle would have to move every input row before any aggregation happened — for 12M rows × ~50 bytes each, that is ~600 MB shuffled instead of ~few KB shuffled. The two-stage plan structure is therefore the *normal* shape, not an optimization that sometimes appears.

The key requirement for the partial stage to apply is that the aggregate function has a mergeable buffer — a state object that can be combined associatively across partitions. sum, count, avg (held internally as sum + count), min, max, stddev — all have this property. collect_list and collect_set technically do (concatenation is associative) but the partial benefit is small because the buffer doesn't shrink.

Functions that *don't* have mergeable buffers (a custom UDAF without a merge() implementation, or percentile_approx with an accumulator that requires merge logic Catalyst cannot prove) suppress the partial stage — and the plan shows only one HashAggregate. JAI3.3 covers that case in detail.

AQE is not involved here. The partial+final pattern is a Catalyst rewrite applied during logical-plan optimization, before any runtime statistics are gathered.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Reads two HashAggregates as duplicate work. They are doing different work — partial_sum within partitions and sum across partitions — and removing one would either explode shuffle volume (if you remove the partial) or produce wrong results (if you remove the final, the partial sums would be the output and never get merged).

Option B: Inverts the execution order. Plans print top-down for human readability but execute bottom-up — operators read from their children. The lower HashAggregate runs first, before the shuffle.

Option D: Attributes partial aggregation to AQE. Partial aggregation is a Catalyst rule (PartialAggregate), applied in logical optimization, independent of AQE. AQE's contributions are different (CustomShuffleReader, skew handling, dynamic coalesce). Partial-aggregation benefit predates AQE by years.

tasks.psiReference: PSI1 lesson 7 (Partial Aggregation: When It Helps and When It Does Not); PSI1 lesson 8 (Reading the Two-Stage HashAggregate Plan)

tasks.title: Why This Plan Has No Partial Stage

description.context: An engineer is comparing two pipelines that look superficially identical: both groupBy by region and produce one aggregate column per region. Pipeline A uses the built-in sum aggregate. Pipeline B uses a custom UDAF the team wrote last quarter for a 'capped weighted sum' calculation. Both produce correct output on the dev sample. Pipeline B is 8× slower in production. The engineer pulls .explain() for both and is asked to interpret the difference.
description.task: What does the structural difference between the two plans most likely indicate about Pipeline B's performance problem?

evidence.label: df.explain() — physical plan
evidence.content:
== Plan A: df.groupBy('region').agg(sum('daily_kw')) ==
HashAggregate(keys=[region#42], functions=[sum(daily_kw#39)])
+- Exchange hashpartitioning(region#42, 200)
   +- HashAggregate(keys=[region#42], functions=[partial_sum(daily_kw#39)])
      +- FileScan parquet bronze.meters[daily_kw#39, region#42]


== Plan B: df.groupBy('region').agg(capped_weighted_sum('daily_kw', 'weight')) ==
ObjectHashAggregate(keys=[region#42], functions=[capped_weighted_sum(daily_kw#39, weight#44)])
+- Exchange hashpartitioning(region#42, 200)
   +- FileScan parquet bronze.meters[daily_kw#39, region#42, weight#44]

fields.label: What does the structural difference between the two plans most likely indicate about Pipeline B's performance problem?
options: Plan B uses ObjectHashAggregate instead of HashAggregate
Plan B has only one HashAggregate stage
Plan B has the same shuffle cost as Plan A
Plan B's missing partial stage is an AQE optimization: AQE detected that the UDAF's
fields.correctAnswer: Plan B has only one HashAggregate stage
fields.rationale:
The diagnostic signal is the absence of the second HashAggregate (partial) stage between the FileScan and the Exchange in Plan B. Reading bottom-up:

- **Plan A** (built-in sum): FileScan → partial HashAggregate → Exchange → final HashAggregate. The partial stage reduces 12M rows to (input_partitions × ~4 regions) rows before the shuffle.

- **Plan B** (custom UDAF): FileScan → Exchange → ObjectHashAggregate. There is no partial stage. Every one of the 12M input rows is hashed by region and shuffled across the network. After the shuffle, the single ObjectHashAggregate collects all rows per region on one node and runs the UDAF over them.

The reason Catalyst skipped the partial stage: the UDAF's accumulator must be mergeable for partial aggregation to apply. 'Mergeable' means there is an associative `merge(buf1, buf2) → buf` operation that combines two partial accumulators into one with the same semantics as a single accumulator over the union of their inputs. Built-in aggregates have this — sum's buffer is a number, merge is addition. UDAFs need to implement merge() explicitly; if the implementation is missing, marked non-deterministic, or has logic Catalyst cannot prove associative, Catalyst declines to apply partial aggregation.

The ObjectHashAggregate operator (instead of HashAggregate) is a hint that the buffer is a generic Object (typically a JVM object holding the UDAF's internal state) rather than a typed primitive — Catalyst falls back to ObjectHashAggregate for UDAFs because it cannot use the optimized HashAggregate code paths for arbitrary buffer types. The operator difference correlates with the buffer mergeability difference but is not itself the cause of the slowdown.

The canonical mid-level fix for this exact pattern (capped weighted sum) is to express the calculation in terms of built-in aggregates:

  result = sum(daily_kw * weight) / sum(weight)   -- weighted average
  capped_result = least(lit(CAP), result)         -- capping post-aggregation

Both sum operations use the partial-aggregation combiner; the cap is applied after aggregation to one row per group, so it is essentially free. This refactor recovers the 8× — typically more, because the original UDAF also pays Python serialization overhead per row.

The pedagogical point: custom aggregators are appealing because they let the engineer express domain logic directly. They are also a scaling trap — a UDAF that runs in 2 minutes on the 100K-row dev sample can run in 45 minutes in production not because the per-row cost grew but because the partial-aggregation rescue does not apply.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Treats ObjectHashAggregate as inherently slow rather than as a hint about buffer type. ObjectHashAggregate is not significantly slower than HashAggregate when both have a partial stage — the slowness in Plan B comes from the missing partial, not from the operator type. Switching to a UDF (instead of UDAF) would not help; UDFs run row-by-row and are generally slower than UDAFs in aggregation contexts.

Option C: Misidentifies the cost driver. Per-row UDAF serialization is real but second-order. The dominant cost is the shuffle volume — 12M rows × ~50 bytes each ≈ 600 MB of network I/O in Plan B vs ~tens of KB in Plan A. The per-row UDAF cost matters once the shuffle has delivered the rows, not before.

Option D: Mis-attributes a Catalyst behavior to AQE. AQE does not skip partial-aggregation stages. The decision to apply partial aggregation is made during logical plan optimization, before AQE has any runtime information. AQE's interventions are post-shuffle (CustomShuffleReader, coalesce, skew handling).

tasks.psiReference: PSI1 lesson 9 (Aggregate Functions and Mergeable Buffers); PSI3 lesson 7 (UDAFs and Combiner Suppression)

tasks.title: Custom Aggregator Disabled the Combiner

description.context: An engineer needs to compute a 'weighted average daily_kw per substation', weighted by an internal trust score the team assigns to each meter (`weight` column). They write a custom Aggregator class. The pipeline runs in 4 minutes on the 100K-row dev sample. In production on the 12M-row dataset it takes 47 minutes. A teammate's pipeline running plain avg('daily_kw') on the same dataset finishes in 90 seconds. Find the structural bug.
description.task: Which line(s) contain the bug, and what is the underlying mechanism?

evidence.label: snippet.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import udf, collect_list, struct, col
from pyspark.sql.types import DoubleType

# Custom 'weighted average' implementation as a Python UDF over a collected list.
@udf(returnType=DoubleType())
def weighted_avg(rows):
    if not rows:
        return None
    total_weight = sum(r['weight'] for r in rows if r['weight'] is not None)
    if total_weight == 0:
        return None
    weighted_total = sum(
        r['daily_kw'] * r['weight']
        for r in rows
        if r['daily_kw'] is not None and r['weight'] is not None
    )
    return weighted_total / total_weight

result = (df
    .filter(col('daily_kw').isNotNull())
    .groupBy('substation_id')
    .agg(collect_list(struct('daily_kw', 'weight')).alias('rows'))
    .withColumn('weighted_avg_kw', weighted_avg(col('rows')))
    .drop('rows'))

evidence.title: Observed behavior
evidence.content: The output values are correct — the operations team verified them against a separate spreadsheet calculation. Runtime on production is 47 minutes; the equivalent built-in avg('daily_kw') takes 90 seconds on the same dataset. Spark UI shows one giant shuffle of ~600 GB before the groupBy stage. There are no errors and no skew warnings.

fields.label: Which line(s) contain the bug, and what is the underlying mechanism?
options: Line 5: the @udf decorator should be @pandas_udf
Lines 23–25: collect_list(struct(...)) → UDF over the array
Line 22: groupBy('substation_id') on 12M rows is the bug
Line 9: the UDF iterates rows with a Python generator
fields.correctAnswer: Lines 23–25: collect_list(struct(...)) → UDF over the array
fields.rationale:
The bug is structural, not algorithmic. The UDF logic is correct (matching the operations team's manual calculation). The problem is the *shape* of the computation:

```
groupBy('substation_id')
  .agg(collect_list(struct('daily_kw', 'weight')))   ← collects every row per substation as a struct array
  .withColumn('weighted_avg_kw', weighted_avg(col('rows')))   ← Python UDF over the array
```

collect_list does have a mergeable buffer (concatenation), so it does benefit from partial aggregation in the structural sense. But the buffer doesn't *shrink* — the partial stage produces partial-array slices that are concatenated post-shuffle into the full array per substation. The shuffle still moves all 12M (daily_kw, weight) tuples, just packaged as arrays. The Spark UI's 600 GB shuffle volume confirms this: input data size is roughly 12M rows × ~50 bytes ≈ 600 MB compressed, which expands several-fold after struct overhead and the array-slice metadata.

The fix is to express the calculation as built-in aggregates that *do* shrink under partial aggregation:

```python
from pyspark.sql.functions import sum as spark_sum, col, when

result = (df
    .filter(col('daily_kw').isNotNull())
    .filter(col('weight').isNotNull())
    .withColumn('kw_x_w', col('daily_kw') * col('weight'))
    .groupBy('substation_id')
    .agg(
        spark_sum('kw_x_w').alias('numer'),
        spark_sum('weight').alias('denom'))
    .withColumn('weighted_avg_kw',
        when(col('denom') == 0, None).otherwise(col('numer') / col('denom')))
    .drop('numer', 'denom'))
```

Both sum aggregates have mergeable scalar buffers. The partial stage reduces 12M rows to (input_partitions × ~120 substations) ≈ a few thousand partial-sum rows before the shuffle. Shuffle volume drops by 4–5 orders of magnitude. The division happens once per substation post-aggregation — essentially free.

This pattern — *re-express the aggregation as sums and divisions on derived columns* — is the canonical mid-level technique for any 'compound' aggregate (weighted averages, ratios, conditional rates, normalized counts). Most calculations that engineers reach for UDAFs to compute can be re-expressed this way, and the re-expression preserves the combiner pattern that UDAFs typically suppress.

The broader principle: a custom aggregation expression that 'works correctly on a sample but is far too slow in production' is almost always a partial-aggregation suppression. The diagnostic signal is shuffle volume comparable to input data size — that means no per-partition reduction happened.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Reaches for vectorization to fix a structural problem. pandas UDFs are faster than regular UDFs per row, but the dominant cost here is the 600 GB shuffle, which vectorization does not address. The UDF could be 10× faster and the shuffle would still dominate.

Option C: Misreads cardinality as the issue. ~120 substations is a fine groupBy cardinality — large enough to parallelize, small enough that per-substation aggregates are tiny. Low cardinality alone is not a problem; what's a problem is shuffling 12M rows of input *into* ~120 groups before reducing them.

Option D: Targets the inner loop of the UDF. Python generators are slow but the UDF runs once per substation (~120 invocations), each over a list of ~100K elements. Total per-row Python cost is bounded; the 47-minute runtime is dominated by shuffle and serialization of the 600 GB intermediate, not by the UDF's interpreter overhead.

tasks.psiReference: PSI1 lesson 9 (Aggregate Functions and Mergeable Buffers); PSI3 lesson 7 (UDAFs and Combiner Suppression)

tasks.title: AQE Coalesce: 200 Partitions Became 8

description.context: An engineer is reading the post-execution physical plan from a successful job. The pipeline groupBys by region (4 distinct values) on the 12M-row meters table and writes the result to Parquet. spark.sql.shuffle.partitions is at the default of 200. AQE is enabled with default settings. The engineer notices the AQEShuffleRead operator and wants to interpret what happened.
description.task: What does AQEShuffleRead coalesced='true', partitions=8 most likely indicate?

evidence.label: df.explain() — physical plan
evidence.content:
== Final Physical Plan (post-AQE) ==
AdaptiveSparkPlan isFinalPlan=true
+- Execute InsertIntoHadoopFsRelationCommand parquet [region]
   +- HashAggregate(keys=[region#42], functions=[finalmerge_sum(...)])
      +- AQEShuffleRead coalesced='true', partitions=8
         +- ShuffleQueryStage 1
            +- Exchange hashpartitioning(region#42, 200), ENSURE_REQUIREMENTS
               +- HashAggregate(keys=[region#42], functions=[partial_sum(daily_kw#39)])
                  +- FileScan parquet bronze.meters[daily_kw#39, region#42]

AQE ShuffleStats:
  Pre-coalesce partitions: 200
  Post-coalesce partitions: 8
  Total shuffle output size: 18 MB
  spark.sql.adaptive.advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes: 64 MB (default)

fields.label: What does AQEShuffleRead coalesced='true', partitions=8 most likely indicate?
options: AQE failed to read 192 of the original 200 partitions; only 8 partitions were successfully read.
The engineer manually set spark.sql.shuffle.partitions=8 for this job.
AQE detected that the shuffle output (18 MB total) was small relative to the
AQE always coalesces to 8 partitions when it engages
fields.correctAnswer: AQE detected that the shuffle output (18 MB total) was small relative to the
fields.rationale:
AQEShuffleRead coalesced='true' is the post-execution marker that AQE's coalesce-shuffle-partitions rule successfully applied. The mechanics:

1. The engineer's code shuffles by region into 200 partitions (the configured spark.sql.shuffle.partitions). This is the planned partition count.
2. After the partial-aggregation stage runs, AQE inspects the materialized shuffle map output. It sees 200 partitions totaling 18 MB — average ~90 KB per partition, far below the advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes target of 64 MB.
3. AQE merges adjacent partitions until each merged partition is roughly advisoryPartitionSize. With 18 MB total and 64 MB target, AQE reasons: 'we want fewer, larger reads; fewer than 1 partition is impossible, so coalesce as far as we can while leaving room for parallelism'. The actual algorithm targets the advisory size but also respects a minimum-partition floor (`spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.minPartitionSize` and similar) that prevents over-coalescing tiny shuffles into a single task. The result is 8 partitions in this case — fewer than 200, more than 1.
4. The downstream HashAggregate (final stage) runs as 8 tasks instead of 200. Scheduler overhead drops; per-task work increases.

Why this matters at production scale: 200 partitions × tiny data per partition means most tasks finish in milliseconds and the entire stage is dominated by task launch, fetch, and result serialization overhead. AQE coalesce removes that overhead by recognizing that the data does not need 200-way parallelism. For groupBy by a low-cardinality key (4 regions), the post-shuffle data is intrinsically small — coalesce is the right intervention.

Three side facts:
- The exact post-coalesce partition count depends on advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes, the total shuffle size, and the minimum-partition-size config. Different runs on different total sizes will produce different counts.
- AQE coalesce only acts on shuffles where it can prove safety. For aggregation shuffles where the output goes to another aggregate, coalesce is safe. For shuffles feeding a join, AQE coordinates coalesce on both sides.
- AQE coalesce is independent of skew handling. They are two different rules and can both engage on the same plan (skew handling first to split skewed partitions, then coalesce to merge the remaining small ones).
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Reads coalesced='true' as a partial failure flag. coalesced='true' is the success indicator — AQE merged partitions intentionally. AQE failures appear as task failures in the executor log, not as coalesced markers.

Option B: Conflates spark.sql.shuffle.partitions (the planned count) with the post-AQE count. The plan shows Exchange hashpartitioning(region, 200), so 200 was the planned count. The 8 came from AQE coalesce.

Option D: Imagines a hardcoded coalesce target. The post-coalesce count is derived from advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes and the actual shuffle size. Different inputs produce different counts; 8 is not magic.

tasks.psiReference: PSI3 lesson 10 (AQE Coalesce: When and How); PSI1 lesson 11 (Reading the Post-AQE Plan)

tasks.title: AQE Skew Join: One Side Was Split

description.context: An engineer is reviewing the post-AQE plan from a join-heavy aggregation pipeline. The pipeline joins the 12M-row meters table to a 5M-row 'meter_history' fact table on meter_id, then groupBys by region. Some meter_ids have far more history rows than others — a few high-traffic industrial meters account for the majority of the history. AQE is enabled with skew handling configured. The engineer sees the plan excerpt below.
description.task: What did AQE do here, and which side has the skew?

evidence.label: df.explain() — physical plan
evidence.content:
== Final Physical Plan (post-AQE) ==
AdaptiveSparkPlan isFinalPlan=true
+- HashAggregate(keys=[region#42], functions=[finalmerge_count(meter_id)])
   +- Exchange hashpartitioning(region#42, 200)
      +- HashAggregate(keys=[region#42], functions=[partial_count(meter_id)])
         +- SortMergeJoin [meter_id#37], [meter_id#101], Inner, skewJoin=true
            :- Sort [meter_id#37 ASC NULLS FIRST]
            :  +- AQEShuffleRead coalesced='false', skewSubpartitions=24
            :     +- ShuffleQueryStage 0
            :        +- Exchange hashpartitioning(meter_id#37, 200)
            :           +- FileScan parquet bronze.meters
            +- Sort [meter_id#101 ASC NULLS FIRST]
               +- AQEShuffleRead coalesced='false'
                  +- ShuffleQueryStage 1
                     +- Exchange hashpartitioning(meter_id#101, 200)
                        +- FileScan parquet silver.meter_history

AQE Skew Stats:
  Skewed partitions on left side: 3 partitions, each split into 8 sub-partitions
  Skewed partitions on right side: none
  Median partition size: 6 MB; skewed partition sizes: 1.2 GB, 0.9 GB, 0.7 GB

fields.label: What did AQE do here, and which side has the skew?
options: AQE coalesced both sides of the join from 200 partitions down to 24.
AQE detected skew on the left (meters) side
AQE detected skew on the right (history) side.
AQE failed to handle the skew
fields.correctAnswer: AQE detected skew on the left (meters) side
fields.rationale:
AQE skew-join handling is the canonical mid-level AQE behavior — and the one most often misread. The mechanics:

1. **Detection**: After the shuffle stages complete, AQE inspects per-partition sizes on each side of the upcoming SortMergeJoin. A partition is flagged 'skewed' when its size exceeds:
   - `spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionFactor` × median partition size (default 5×), AND
   - `spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionThresholdInBytes` (default 256 MB).
   Partitions of 1.2 GB / 0.9 GB / 0.7 GB on a 6 MB median pass both checks easily; the 3 left-side partitions are flagged.

2. **Splitting**: AQE splits each skewed partition into N sub-partitions (here N=8 — controlled by the advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes target). The 1.2 GB partition becomes 8 × 150 MB sub-partitions; the others split similarly. Total sub-partitions: 24, exactly matching the plan marker `skewSubpartitions=24`.

3. **Replication on the unskewed side**: For each skewed partition that AQE splits, the matching partition on the other side must be sent to *every* sub-partition (because the join requires both sides' data for the same key range). AQE replicates the relevant right-side partition's rows so each left-side sub-task has what it needs. This is the cost of skew-join handling — extra duplication of right-side data — and it pays off when skew is severe enough that without splitting, one task would block the entire stage.

4. **The 197 unskewed partitions** on the left side run unchanged. AQE only intervenes where intervention helps.

The fingerprint to recognize:
- `SortMergeJoin(..., skewJoin=true)` → AQE engaged its skew handler.
- `AQEShuffleRead coalesced='false', skewSubpartitions=N` → that side had partitions split.
- `AQEShuffleRead coalesced='false'` (no skew marker) on the other side → that side was unskewed and its matching partitions were replicated to feed the splits.

The skew is on the *left* (meters) side because that is where skewSubpartitions appears. AQE-skew-join targets the side that is too big for one task; the other side's data is duplicated to feed the splits.

Why mid-level engineers misread this: 'skew' colloquially means 'data is unevenly distributed', and a junior reading might attribute it to whichever side has 'more rows'. The technical definition is precise — skew is per-partition imbalance after the shuffle. The skewed side is whichever side has a partition ≥ skewedPartitionFactor × median.

Note: skew-join handling is *different* from coalesce. Coalesce merges small partitions into fewer large ones; skew-join splits one large partition into smaller ones. They can both apply on the same plan to different stages.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Conflates skew handling with coalesce. They are two different AQE rules. The marker name `skewSubpartitions` (not `coalescedPartitions`) and the explicit `coalesced='false'` distinguish them in this plan.

Option C: Confuses the side with skew. The skewSubpartitions marker is on the left side's AQEShuffleRead, not the right's. The right side was the one duplicated to match — but the plan's marker is on the side that was *split*, not the side that was *replicated*.

Option D: Reads skewJoin=true as a failure flag rather than a success flag. skewJoin=true means AQE engaged its skew handler — the success case. AQE failure to handle skew would simply produce a slow stage with no skew markers in the post-execution plan.

tasks.psiReference: PSI3 lesson 11 (AQE Skew Join: Split-and-Replicate); PSI3 lesson 12 (Configuring AQE Skew Thresholds)

tasks.title: AQE Disabled — Skewed Pipeline Suffers

description.context: An engineer is debugging a slow aggregation. The pipeline groupBys 12M meter readings by customer_type (60% RESIDENTIAL, 25% COMMERCIAL, 12% INDUSTRIAL, 3% PROSUMER) and computes a sum. The job runs in 28 minutes. The engineer wrote no salting code; they expected 'AQE handles skew automatically'. A colleague's structurally similar pipeline on the same cluster runs in 4 minutes. The engineer is asked to find the bug. The full code is shown including the SparkSession setup.
description.task: Which line contains the bug, and what is the underlying mechanism?

evidence.label: snippet.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import sum, col

spark = (SparkSession.builder
    .appName('customer_type_aggregation')
    .config('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions', '200')
    .config('spark.sql.adaptive.enabled', 'false')
    .config('spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold', 10 * 1024 * 1024)
    .getOrCreate())

meters = spark.read.parquet('bronze/meters/2026-04-*/')

result = (meters
    .filter(col('customer_type').isNotNull())
    .groupBy('customer_type')
    .agg(sum('daily_kw').alias('total_kw')))

result.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('gold/customer_type_totals/')

evidence.title: Observed behavior
evidence.content: The job runs successfully and produces correct totals. Runtime: 28 minutes. The Spark UI shows 200 tasks in the final aggregation stage, with task durations of 14 min (1 task), 6 min (1 task), 3 min (1 task), 90 sec (1 task), and < 5 sec (196 tasks). The single 14-minute task ran the entire stage's critical path. AQE skew handling did not engage; coalesce did not engage. A colleague's similar pipeline running on the same cluster (different Spark session) runs in 4 minutes.

fields.label: Which line contains the bug, and what is the underlying mechanism?
options: Line 7: spark.sql.adaptive.enabled is set to 'false', disabling AQE entirely.
Line 6: spark.sql.shuffle.partitions=200 is too high for a 4-key groupBy.
Line 16: groupBy('customer_type') is the bug
Line 18: write.mode('overwrite') triggers a recursive directory delete that takes 24 of the 28 minutes.
fields.correctAnswer: Line 7: spark.sql.adaptive.enabled is set to 'false', disabling AQE entirely.
fields.rationale:
The bug is the `spark.sql.adaptive.enabled = 'false'` config on line 7. AQE is disabled, so all of AQE's runtime interventions — skew-join handling, coalesce, dynamic broadcast switch — never run. The pipeline's behavior with AQE off:

1. The shuffle hashpartitions all 12M rows by customer_type into 200 partitions.
2. Hash partitioning sends every row of the same customer_type to the same partition. With 4 distinct keys, only 4 of 200 partitions receive data.
3. The largest partition (RESIDENTIAL, 60% of rows = 7.2M rows) runs as a single task. This is the 14-minute task in Spark UI.
4. The other 3 partitions run in parallel but the stage cannot finish until the 14-minute task completes. 'Stragglers' is the operational term.

With AQE enabled (the default in Spark 3.5+ for most distributions, and the colleague's setting), the same pipeline benefits from two interventions:

1. **Skew-join handling does not apply** here directly because there is no join — but the analogous mechanism for groupBy aggregation is partition-level skew detection. With AQE on, AQE *can* detect the imbalance, but for pure groupBy by a low-cardinality key, AQE cannot split a single key's rows across partitions (the final aggregate must collocate all rows for the same key). The genuine fix at the engineer level is two-stage salting (groupBy by customer_type + salt → groupBy by customer_type) — see JAI2.3.

2. **AQE coalesce** would in fact engage on the upper stage and merge the 196 empty/near-empty partitions into a few, reducing scheduler overhead modestly. This isn't the dominant fix but contributes — and only happens if AQE is enabled.

3. The colleague's 4-minute pipeline likely uses salting *plus* AQE — both interventions stack. The bug here is that disabling AQE forecloses the second of those interventions and any future automatic ones.

Why the engineer's reasoning ('AQE handles skew automatically') is half-right and half-wrong: AQE skew handling is automatic *for joins*. For pure aggregations on low-cardinality skewed keys, AQE has limited tools (coalesce won't split keys; skew-handling targets joins). The engineer needs to apply manual salting. But disabling AQE on top of that takes away even the coalesce benefit and any future AQE improvements.

The immediate fix: remove line 7 (or set it to 'true'). The more complete fix: also add salting for the groupBy by customer_type, because AQE alone cannot solve key-cardinality skew in pure aggregations.

Reading discipline: when a mid-level engineer reads a SparkSession setup, they should skim explicit `spark.sql.adaptive.*` configs as carefully as they read the aggregation code. AQE-disabling configs are almost always inherited from older codebases or copy-pasted from blog posts; they almost always reduce performance on modern Spark.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option B: Believes that matching shuffle.partitions to key cardinality fixes skew. It does not. Hash partitioning is deterministic — RESIDENTIAL still ends up on one partition regardless of total partition count. Setting partitions to 4 just means the other 3 keys also each get one partition, which they would have under the default anyway.

Option C: Targets groupBy as the structural problem. groupBy by customer_type is correct for the stated business goal (per-customer-type total). Low cardinality is the *cause* of the skew, not a wrong choice — and the fix is salting + AQE, not switching to a different operator.

Option D: Reaches for a write-side explanation. Spark UI shows the 14-minute task in the aggregation stage, before the write begins. write.mode('overwrite') replaces a directory atomically; it does not take 24 minutes.

tasks.psiReference: PSI3 lesson 12 (Configuring AQE Skew Thresholds); PSI3 lesson 13 (When AQE Cannot Help: Pure Aggregation Skew)

tasks.title: AQE Tuned Wrong: Too Few, Too Big

description.context: An engineer copied a 'big data tuning' config block from a 2019 internal wiki page and applied it to NordGrid's daily aggregation pipeline. The pipeline aggregates 12M meter readings, joins to substations, and writes Parquet. AQE is enabled. The pipeline runs but on most days one executor OOMs and the stage retries (eventually completing on retry, but adding 8–10 minutes of wasted work). The engineer is asked to find the bug. The full Spark config is shown.
description.task: Which line contains the bug, and what is the underlying mechanism?

evidence.label: snippet.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import sum, col

spark = (SparkSession.builder
    .appName('daily_aggregation')
    .config('spark.sql.adaptive.enabled', 'true')
    .config('spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled', 'true')
    .config('spark.sql.adaptive.advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes', 1024 * 1024 * 1024)
    .config('spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled', 'true')
    .config('spark.executor.memory', '8g')
    .config('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions', '200')
    .getOrCreate())

meters = spark.read.parquet('bronze/meters/2026-04-*/')
substations = spark.read.parquet('silver/substations/')

result = (meters
    .join(substations, on='substation_id', how='inner')
    .groupBy('region', 'customer_type')
    .agg(sum('daily_kw').alias('total_kw')))

result.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('gold/daily_aggregation/')

evidence.title: Observed behavior
evidence.content: The pipeline runs but on most days one executor OOMs in the final aggregation stage. Spark retries the stage and it eventually completes on retry (a different executor, with the same partition layout, succeeds — sometimes). Total wall-clock time including retry is 8–10 minutes longer than expected. AQE is engaged: the post-execution plan shows AQEShuffleRead coalesced='true' partitions=6 on the final shuffle. customer_type distribution is the same skewed shape as before (60/25/12/3).

fields.label: Which line contains the bug, and what is the underlying mechanism?
options: Line 8: advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes = 1 GB is too aggressive.
Line 9: skewJoin.enabled='true' should be 'false' for groupBy operations.
Line 11: spark.executor.memory at 8g is too low.
Line 7: coalescePartitions.enabled should be 'false'
fields.correctAnswer: Line 8: advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes = 1 GB is too aggressive.
fields.rationale:
The bug is line 8: advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes set to 1 GB. The 2019-era 'big data' wisdom (large partitions reduce scheduler overhead) does not interact well with skewed data, and AQE's coalesce honors the advisory size aggressively.

What actually happens:

1. The shuffle plans 200 partitions (per shuffle.partitions=200). For an unskewed dataset with ~12 GB total post-shuffle data, each partition would hold ~60 MB.

2. With a 60/25/12/3 skew on customer_type, the post-shuffle data is *already* unbalanced before AQE acts: ~7.2 GB on RESIDENTIAL, ~3 GB on COMMERCIAL, ~1.4 GB on INDUSTRIAL, ~0.4 GB on PROSUMER, with the rest (12 keys × 4 regions = 48 logical groups, but most concentrated on 4 customer_type buckets after hashing) spread thin.

3. AQE coalesce reads the per-partition sizes and merges adjacent partitions toward the 1 GB advisory target. Most of the small partitions (the 196 near-empty ones) get merged into a few of the larger ones. The 6 final partitions reported in the post-execution plan reflect this aggressive merging.

4. One of those 6 coalesced partitions inherits the entire RESIDENTIAL bucket — ~7.2 GB. The advisory size of 1 GB is supposed to be a target ceiling, but coalesce cannot split a single skewed partition (only skew-join handling does that, and only on join shuffles). So one partition holds 7.2 GB of post-shuffle data.

5. The executor reading that partition has 8 GB total memory. With Spark's memory overhead, only ~5 GB is available for shuffle-read buffers. Holding 7.2 GB of data plus aggregation state OOMs the executor.

Why a smaller advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes helps: with the default ~64 MB, AQE coalesces less aggressively. The 196 small partitions still merge, but the merging stops when partitions reach ~64 MB. The RESIDENTIAL partition stays close to its native ~7.2 GB / 200 = 36 MB per shuffle slot, spread across roughly 200 partitions of which the populated ones average 36 MB. The 7.2 GB of RESIDENTIAL data is still concentrated on one logical partition (because hash partitioning sends every RESIDENTIAL row to the same partition), but coalescing other small partitions into it does not happen — the advisory target stops the merge.

The interaction with skew-join handling: skew-join handling only acts on join shuffles. The skewed partition here is the *aggregation* shuffle, where the hash-by-customer_type cannot be split (every row of the same customer_type must collocate for the final HashAggregate). AQE has no rescue for this. The combination of (a) inherent groupBy-on-low-cardinality skew + (b) coalesce-too-aggressive lets a 7.2 GB partition reach a single 8 GB executor.
