The canonical mid-level fixes are (1) lower advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes back toward defaults — typically 64 MB, sometimes 128 MB on memory-rich clusters — and (2) apply manual salting on customer_type to spread RESIDENTIAL across multiple partitions. With both fixes, AQE coalesce continues merging the small unsalted parts, the salted parts run in parallel, and no single partition reaches OOM size.

The broader principle: AQE parameters interact. coalesce-aggressive defeats the natural anti-skew benefit of having many partitions; skew handling has limited reach for pure aggregations. Tuning AQE without considering these interactions makes some pipelines faster and others worse — copying configs from blog posts is exactly how engineers end up here.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option B: Believes skew-join handling interferes with groupBy. Skew-join handling has no effect on aggregation shuffles — it only acts on shuffles feeding a SortMergeJoin. Disabling it would not change this pipeline's behavior.

Option C: Targets executor memory rather than the partition-size root cause. Doubling memory from 8 GB to 16 GB would mask the bug for now but the partition is 7.2 GB and growing — six months later when the dataset is larger the same OOM returns. Fixing partition layout is the correct level.

Option D: Treats coalesce and skew-join as mutually exclusive. They are independent rules and apply to different shuffles. Disabling coalesce would mean the 196 small partitions stay separate (more scheduler overhead for the unskewed parts) without addressing the 7.2 GB skewed partition at all.

tasks.psiReference: PSI3 lesson 10 (AQE Coalesce: When and How); PSI3 lesson 13 (When AQE Cannot Help: Pure Aggregation Skew); PSI3 lesson 14 (Tuning advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes)

tasks.title: rowsBetween vs rangeBetween: Gap Day Disagreement

description.context: An engineer is reviewing two snippets that both compute a 'rolling 7-day average' of daily_kw per region for an operations dashboard. Both run on the 12M-row meters table. The business stakeholder has stated the goal as 'the average daily consumption over the last 7 calendar days, ending today'. The engineer needs to pick which definition matches that goal — and identify what the other one actually computes when meters have gap days (no reading on a given date).
description.task: Both snippets work correctly when every meter has a reading every day. They diverge when a meter has gap days. Which one matches the stated business goal of 'last 7 calendar days', and what does the other one actually compute?

evidence.title: Goal
evidence.content: For each (meter_id, date) row in a per-meter time series, compute the average daily_kw over the last 7 calendar days, ending on the current date.

evidence.label: Snippet A — rowsBetween(-6, 0)
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import Window
from pyspark.sql.functions import avg, col

w = (Window
    .partitionBy('meter_id')
    .orderBy('date')
    .rowsBetween(-6, 0))

result_a = df.withColumn('rolling_7d_avg', avg('daily_kw').over(w))

evidence.label: Snippet B — rangeBetween over date-as-long
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import Window
from pyspark.sql.functions import avg, col, unix_timestamp

df_with_ts = df.withColumn('date_long', unix_timestamp(col('date')).cast('long'))

w = (Window
    .partitionBy('meter_id')
    .orderBy('date_long')
    .rangeBetween(-6 * 86400, 0))

result_b = df_with_ts.withColumn('rolling_7d_avg', avg('daily_kw').over(w))

fields.label: Both snippets work correctly when every meter has a reading every day. They diverge when a meter has gap days. Which one matches the stated business goal of 'last 7 calendar days', and what does the other one actually compute?
options: Both produce the same result on any input
Snippet A matches the goal. rowsBetween counts calendar days correctly because dates are
Snippet B matches the goal. rangeBetween(-6 * 86400, 0) over a unix-timestamp column
Neither matches the goal
fields.correctAnswer: Snippet B matches the goal. rangeBetween(-6 * 86400, 0) over a unix-timestamp column
fields.rationale:
The two operators frame the window in fundamentally different ways:

**rowsBetween(-6, 0)** counts *rows* — the previous 6 rows plus the current row, regardless of what dates they cover. If a meter has a reading every day for 30 days and you compute over date 2026-04-30, the window covers rows 2026-04-24 through 2026-04-30 — exactly 7 calendar days. But if the meter went offline on 2026-04-25 (no row for that date), the window for 2026-04-30 covers rows 2026-04-23, 2026-04-24, 2026-04-26, 2026-04-27, 2026-04-28, 2026-04-29, 2026-04-30 — 7 rows spanning 8 calendar days. The window 'reached back further' to fill the row count.

**rangeBetween(-6 * 86400, 0)** with unix-timestamp ordering frames by *value range*. For a row at timestamp T, the window covers all rows with timestamp in [T - 6×86400, T] — the last 7 × 86400 seconds = exactly 7 days inclusive. If the meter has a gap day, the window for 2026-04-30 covers timestamps from 2026-04-24 00:00:00 to 2026-04-30 23:59:59 — and includes whatever rows are present in that range (here, 6 rows, missing 2026-04-25). The average is over 6 readings instead of 7.

The stated goal — 'the average over the last 7 calendar days' — is rangeBetween's semantics: the window is defined by calendar-time, and missing days simply mean fewer readings in the average rather than reaching back further to fill quota. Snippet B is correct.

Snippet A's behavior — reaching back further when there are gaps — is occasionally what an engineer wants (e.g., 'the average over the last 7 readings, regardless of how recent') but is not what 'last 7 calendar days' means. On meters with frequent gaps, Snippet A produces averages that mix readings from very different time periods, which is wrong for trend tracking.

The trap that catches mid-level engineers: on a clean dev sample where every meter has a reading every day, Snippets A and B produce identical output. The bug surfaces only on production data with realistic gaps. The reading discipline: when a snippet uses rowsBetween(-N+1, 0), ask 'is the row count or the date range what the business question is asking about?' The two are equivalent only when the data has no gaps.

A subtle implementation note: rangeBetween over a unix-timestamp column requires the timestamp to be cast to long (or an integer-typed column) for Spark to apply the range frame. Casting Date to long via unix_timestamp is the canonical idiom; the multiplier 86400 (seconds per day) makes the range expression read clearly. PySpark also supports rangeBetween over a Date column directly in some versions but the long-cast version is portable across Spark versions.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Reads rowsBetween and rangeBetween as syntactic variants. They are different operators with different semantics; the equivalence on uniformly-spaced data is coincidental, not by design.

Option B: Inverts the answer. rowsBetween counts rows, not calendar days. rangeBetween over unix timestamps counts calendar seconds, which (for the chosen range of 6×86400 seconds) corresponds to exactly 6 calendar days back — modulo daylight-saving-time edge cases that are negligible at daily granularity. The 'month-length' concern is irrelevant because the multiplier specifies a fixed-second range, not a calendar offset.

Option D: Imagines that range windows cannot be expressed in PySpark. They can — rangeBetween over an ordered numeric column is exactly the operator for calendar-range windows. Self-joins are the workaround when window functions are not available, not when they are.

tasks.psiReference: PSI1 lesson 5 (Frame Semantics: rowsBetween and rangeBetween); PSI1 lesson 4 (Window Functions in the Plan)

tasks.title: Sessionization: Per-Meter Partition vs Cross-Meter Bug

description.context: An engineer is reviewing two snippets that assign 'session IDs' to meter readings. A session is defined as a sequence of readings from the same meter where consecutive readings are no more than 30 minutes apart. A gap of more than 30 minutes starts a new session. Both snippets process a 12M-row stream of meter readings and produce a session_id column. The output looks plausible at a glance — both produce non-null integer session IDs. The engineer needs to identify which one correctly assigns sessions per meter, and what is wrong with the other.
description.task: Both snippets compute is_new_session correctly per meter. They differ in how they assign session_id from is_new_session. Which is correct, and what does the other actually compute?

evidence.title: Goal
evidence.content: For each meter reading, assign a session_id integer such that consecutive readings within 30 minutes of each other share the same session_id, and a gap of more than 30 minutes starts a new session. Sessions are independent per meter — meter A's sessions must not share IDs with meter B's session IDs across the dataset.

evidence.label: Snippet A — gap detection partitioned by meter, cumulative sum over the same partition
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import Window
from pyspark.sql.functions import lag, col, when, sum, lit

w_meter = Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy('ts')

df2 = df.withColumn('prev_ts', lag('ts').over(w_meter))

df2 = df2.withColumn('is_new_session',
    when(col('prev_ts').isNull(), lit(1))
    .when((col('ts').cast('long') - col('prev_ts').cast('long')) > 30 * 60, lit(1))
    .otherwise(lit(0)))

result_a = df2.withColumn('session_id',
    sum('is_new_session').over(
        Window.partitionBy('meter_id')
            .orderBy('ts')
            .rowsBetween(Window.unboundedPreceding, Window.currentRow)))

evidence.label: Snippet B — gap detection partitioned by meter, cumulative sum over a global window
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import Window
from pyspark.sql.functions import lag, col, when, sum, lit

w_meter = Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy('ts')

df2 = df.withColumn('prev_ts', lag('ts').over(w_meter))

df2 = df2.withColumn('is_new_session',
    when(col('prev_ts').isNull(), lit(1))
    .when((col('ts').cast('long') - col('prev_ts').cast('long')) > 30 * 60, lit(1))
    .otherwise(lit(0)))

result_b = df2.withColumn('session_id',
    sum('is_new_session').over(
        Window.orderBy('ts')
            .rowsBetween(Window.unboundedPreceding, Window.currentRow)))

fields.label: Both snippets compute is_new_session correctly per meter. They differ in how they assign session_id from is_new_session. Which is correct, and what does the other actually compute?
options: Both are correct
Snippet A is correct.
Snippet B is correct.
Both have the same bug
fields.correctAnswer: Snippet A is correct.
fields.rationale:
Both snippets compute the *gap-detection* column (is_new_session) correctly using lag() partitioned by meter_id. The bug is in the second window — the cumulative sum that turns gap markers into session IDs.

**Snippet A** uses Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy('ts') for the cumulative sum. Within each meter's partition, is_new_session is 1 at the first row and at every gap-greater-than-30-minutes; the cumulative sum increments by 1 at those points and stays flat in between. Result: meter A gets session IDs 1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 3 (a meter with three sessions of varying length). Meter B independently gets 1, 1, 2 (a meter with two sessions). The session IDs are *per-meter integers* — meter A's session 1 and meter B's session 1 are different sessions that happen to share the integer 1.

This is the canonical sessionization output. Downstream pipelines compose the unique session identifier as (meter_id, session_id) — the meter_id disambiguates the integer.

**Snippet B** uses Window.orderBy('ts') without partitionBy. The cumulative sum runs across *every row in the dataset* in timestamp order, regardless of meter. is_new_session=1 markers from any meter all increment the same global counter. The output has two distinct problems:

1. **Cross-meter session IDs leak**: a single integer session_id applies to readings from many different meters whose session-boundaries happened to occur near each other in time. The session_id no longer has the per-meter semantic the goal requires.

2. **Within-meter session IDs are nondeterministic in their numeric value**: meter A's session 1 might be session_id=4 (because three other meters had session-starts before meter A's first reading). Re-running the pipeline with a different processing order or a different shuffle.partitions setting can produce different session_id values for the same logical session. The per-meter session boundaries are still correct, but the IDs are unstable and meaningless.

3. **Practical performance disaster**: a Window without partitionBy means the entire dataset must funnel through a single executor for the cumulative sum stage. At 12M rows this is essentially fatal. (Spark will warn 'No Partition Defined for Window operation' with a logger note about performance — but the warning does not prevent execution.)

The pedagogical point: the partitionBy of the cumulative-sum window must match the partitionBy of the gap-detection window. Forgetting partitionBy on the second window — which is exactly what an engineer does when copying the gap-detection idiom from a tutorial that omits it — is the canonical sessionization bug.

The `Window.unboundedPreceding to Window.currentRow` frame is correct in both snippets — it makes the cumulative sum cumulative-up-to-and-including the current row, which is what session-ID assignment requires.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Treats partitionBy as a performance toggle rather than as semantically meaningful. partitionBy is part of the window's identity — different partitionBy clauses define different windows and produce different aggregations.

Option C: Believes that 'globally unique IDs' is the goal. The stated goal is per-meter sessions; uniqueness across meters is not required and is achieved at the composition level (meter_id, session_id). The 'global session_id' Snippet B produces does not even achieve global uniqueness reliably — it depends on processing order.

Option D: Misremembers the unboundedPreceding rule. unbounded preceding is allowed with all aggregate functions; some functions (like row_number, rank) require an explicit orderBy but unboundedPreceding is fine. Both snippets run without error.

tasks.psiReference: PSI1 lesson 4 (Window Functions in the Plan); PSI1 lesson 5 (Frame Semantics: rowsBetween and rangeBetween)

tasks.title: Tumbling Window Output Schema

description.context: An engineer is reading a snippet that aggregates meter readings into 5-minute tumbling time windows for a real-time monitoring dashboard. They want to predict the output schema before plumbing the result into a downstream consumer that will index by window.
description.task: What does `tumbled.printSchema()` print?

evidence.label: snippet.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import window, sum, col

tumbled = (df
    .groupBy(
        window(col('ts'), '5 minutes'),
        col('meter_id'))
    .agg(sum('daily_kw').alias('window_kw')))

tumbled.printSchema()

fields.label: What does `tumbled.printSchema()` print?
options:
root
 |-- window_start: timestamp (nullable = true)
 |-- window_end: timestamp (nullable =

root
 |-- window: string (nullable = true)
 |-- meter_id: string (nullable = true)
 |-- window_kw: double

root
 |-- window: struct (nullable = true)
 |    |-- start: timestamp (nullable = true)
 |

root
 |-- window: array<timestamp> (nullable = true)
 |-- meter_id: string (nullable =
fields.correctAnswer:
root
 |-- window: struct (nullable = true)
 |    |-- start: timestamp (nullable = true)
 |
fields.rationale:
PySpark's `window(col, duration)` function returns a *struct column* containing two timestamp fields: `start` and `end`. The struct represents a half-open interval [start, end). When grouping by `window(...)`, the resulting DataFrame's first column is named `window` and has type `struct<start: timestamp, end: timestamp>`. To access the boundaries, downstream code uses `col('window.start')` and `col('window.end')`.

This schema convention is the same across all of PySpark's time-windowing functions:
- `window(col, '5 minutes')` — tumbling (fixed-size, non-overlapping)
- `window(col, '5 minutes', '1 minute')` — sliding
- `session_window(col, '5 minutes')` — session (gap-based, returns a similar struct with start/end after session resolution)

Downstream pipelines that want flat columns typically project them out:

```python
tumbled.select(
    col('window.start').alias('window_start'),
    col('window.end').alias('window_end'),
    col('meter_id'),
    col('window_kw')
)
```

The pedagogical point: time windows produce a struct-typed column, not flat columns. Engineers who index the result by the literal column name 'window_start' or 'window_end' get an AnalysisException because those column names do not exist at the top level — they are nested under `window`.

A related gotcha worth knowing: the field is named `start` and `end` (not `window_start` and `window_end`). Catalyst's window operator chose those names; PySpark documentation matches.

Note the `meter_id` column appears with the type from the input (string in NordGrid's schema). The aggregate `window_kw` carries its alias and the result type of `sum` over a double column is double.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Imagines that PySpark flattens the window struct into top-level columns. It does not — the struct is the canonical representation; flattening is the engineer's choice via select(col('window.start').alias(...)).

Option B: Treats the window column as a stringified label. window() returns structured timestamp data; printing it shows two timestamps, not a string.

Option D: Imagines that an interval is represented as an array of two timestamps. Spark uses a struct (with named fields) rather than an array (positional) for window intervals — a deliberate choice that makes downstream code self-documenting via field names rather than array indices.

tasks.psiReference: PSI1 lesson 13 (Time Windows in PySpark: window() and session_window()); PSI1 lesson 14 (The Window Struct Schema)

tasks.title: Late-Arriving Data Pipeline

description.context: You are reviewing a streaming aggregation pipeline that reads meter readings from a Kafka topic, tumbling-windows them by 1 hour, and writes hourly per-region totals to a Delta table. The pipeline runs in production. A downstream consumer reports that 'occasionally a window's aggregate is missing from the output — the row for hour H simply never appears'. The colleague is asking for a code review. Read the full pipeline carefully — line numbers are shown — then answer the questions below.
description.task: Read the pipeline carefully — line numbers are shown — then answer all the questions below.

evidence.label: snippet.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, sum, window, from_json
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StringType, DoubleType, TimestampType

spark = (SparkSession.builder
    .appName('hourly_region_totals')
    .config('spark.sql.adaptive.enabled', 'true')
    .getOrCreate())

schema = (StructType()
    .add('meter_id', StringType())
    .add('daily_kw', DoubleType())
    .add('ts', TimestampType())
    .add('region', StringType()))

raw = (spark.readStream
    .format('kafka')
    .option('kafka.bootstrap.servers', 'kafka:9092')
    .option('subscribe', 'meter_readings')
    .load())

parsed = (raw
    .selectExpr("CAST(value AS STRING) as json_str")
    .select(from_json(col('json_str'), schema).alias('m'))
    .select('m.*'))

withwm = parsed.withWatermark('ts', '5 minutes')

hourly = (withwm
    .groupBy(
        window(col('ts'), '1 hour'),
        col('region'))
    .agg(sum('daily_kw').alias('total_kw')))

query = (hourly.writeStream
    .format('delta')
    .outputMode('append')
    .option('checkpointLocation', '/checkpoints/hourly_region/')
    .option('path', '/gold/hourly_region/')
    .trigger(processingTime='30 seconds')
    .start())

query.awaitTermination()

fields.label: Q1 — What does the watermark on line 27 control?
options: How often Spark checkpoints state to disk
The maximum lateness Spark tolerates before closing a window
The minimum interval between micro-batches
The latency between source clock and Spark cluster clock
fields.correctAnswer: The maximum lateness Spark tolerates before closing a window
fields.rationale: withWatermark('ts', '5 minutes') tells Spark: 'your event-time clock advances as max(ts) advances, but you should consider any record whose ts is more than 5 minutes behind max(ts) to be unrecoverably late'. Operationally, Spark uses the watermark to decide when window state can be retired. Once the watermark advances 5 minutes past a window's end, Spark drops that window's aggregator from in-memory state — freeing memory and signaling that no further updates to that window will be processed.

fields.label: Q2 — Why does `outputMode('append')` on line 41 mean a window's aggregate appears in the output only after the watermark passes the window's end?
options: append mode emits each row exactly once.
append mode requires a Delta table
append mode emits rows immediately and the watermark has no effect on output timing
append mode requires processing-time triggers; the watermark passing the window-end is coincidental
fields.correctAnswer: append mode emits each row exactly once.
fields.rationale: outputMode('append') has a strict contract: every row in the output is final and will never be updated. For a windowed aggregation this is not satisfiable until the window is guaranteed to receive no further updates — i.e., until the watermark has passed the window's end (plus the lateness threshold; Spark uses the watermark to define this point). Until that moment, the window's row is held back. Once the watermark passes, the aggregate is emitted exactly once and never changes. This is why append mode is the canonical mode for windowed aggregations writing to immutable storage like Delta or Parquet.

fields.label: Q3 — A meter reading with event_time T arrives at the cluster long after the watermark for hour H (containing T) has already advanced past H's end. What happens to that record?
options: Spark adds it to the H window's aggregate retroactively and updates the Delta table by
Spark drops the record silently.
Spark routes the record to a quarantine topic for late records, configured by spark.streaming.lateDataPath.
Spark raises a StreamingQueryException and the pipeline fails.
fields.correctAnswer: Spark drops the record silently.
fields.rationale: The state for window H was dropped when the watermark advanced past H + 5 minutes. The arriving record cannot reach a non-existent aggregator. Spark drops the record silently — there is no exception, no quarantine path by default, no log warning at the application level (though Streaming metrics do count 'numRowsDroppedByWatermark'). The reading discipline: streaming aggregates are always the result of a memory/lateness tradeoff. Records arriving after the lateness threshold are lost by design. This is not a bug — it is the explicit contract of withWatermark. The consumer's 'missing windows' report could mean either (a) records that were dropped by watermark, or (b) windows where every record was dropped (so the window itself never had an aggregate to emit).

fields.label: Q4 — Which line would the engineer change to allow more late data to be accepted into hourly aggregates (at the cost of holding window state longer)?
options: Line 31
Line 44
Line 27
Line 41
fields.correctAnswer: Line 27
fields.rationale: The watermark delay (line 27, second argument to withWatermark) is the explicit lateness tolerance. Increasing from '5 minutes' to '1 hour' means windows hold their state for an additional 55 minutes after their end-time before being retired — so records arriving up to an hour late get aggregated correctly. The cost is memory: every active window's aggregator stays resident in state until the watermark passes its end + delay. For 4 regions × 24 hourly windows in flight × an hour of additional retention, the memory cost is small; for a higher-cardinality grouping, it can be substantial. The engineer chooses the delay to match the realistic worst-case lateness for their data source. Window duration (option A) does not change lateness tolerance — it changes window granularity. Trigger interval (option B) controls how often Spark processes batches but does not change which records the watermark accepts. Output mode (option D) is fixed by the downstream contract; switching to 'update' would require the Delta sink to support upserts and changes the consumer's contract entirely.

tasks.psiReference: PSI1 lesson 13 (Time Windows in PySpark: window() and session_window()); PSI1 lesson 15 (Streaming Aggregation: Watermarks and Output Modes)

tasks.title: Daily Substation Report at 12M-Meter Scale

description.context: You are reviewing a production pull request for the daily substation report pipeline at NordGrid Energy. The pipeline reads ~12M meter readings per day from Bronze, joins to a small substations reference table, computes per-substation daily totals and a 7-day rolling average per substation, and writes partitioned Parquet to Silver. The code runs and produces correct output on the staging cluster (8 executors, 4 cores each, 16GB RAM per executor). Read the pipeline carefully — line numbers are shown — and answer the questions below.
description.task: Read the pipeline carefully — line numbers are shown — then answer all the questions below.

evidence.label: snippet.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, sum, avg, to_date, broadcast
from pyspark.sql.window import Window

spark = (SparkSession.builder
    .appName("daily_substation_report")
    .config("spark.sql.adaptive.enabled", "true")
    .config("spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled", "true")
    .config("spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold", "10485760")
    .getOrCreate())

# Bronze: ~12M raw meter readings for the last 30 days
meters = (spark.read
    .option("header", True)
    .option("inferSchema", True)
    .csv("bronze/meters/2026-04-*/"))

meters = meters.withColumn("date", to_date(col("date"), "yyyy-MM-dd"))
meters = meters.filter(col("daily_kw").isNotNull() & (col("daily_kw") >= 0))

# Substations reference: 6 rows, ~2KB total
substations = spark.read.parquet("silver/substations/")

# Enrich with substation metadata
enriched = meters.join(
    broadcast(substations.select("substation_id", "name", "region")),
    on="substation_id",
    how="inner"
)

# Daily total per substation
daily = (enriched
    .groupBy("substation_id", "name", "region", "date")
    .agg(sum("daily_kw").alias("total_kw")))

# 7-day rolling average per substation (calendar days)
w = Window.partitionBy("substation_id").orderBy(col("date").cast("long")).rangeBetween(-6 * 86400, 0)
enriched_daily = daily.withColumn("rolling_7d_avg", avg("total_kw").over(w))

# Final layout for Silver: one file per date partition
final = enriched_daily.repartition("date")

final.write.mode("overwrite").partitionBy("date").parquet("silver/daily_substation_report/")

fields.label: Q1 — The substations table has 6 rows (~2KB). Line 27 wraps it in broadcast(). Given the autoBroadcastJoinThreshold config on line 9 is 10MB, what is the effect of the explicit broadcast() hint here?
options: The hint is essential
The hint is redundant.
The hint is harmful
The hint changes the join from inner to broadcast-inner
fields.correctAnswer: The hint is redundant.
fields.rationale: The hint is redundant at this size. Catalyst's autoBroadcast logic would broadcast a 2KB table without any prompting — the threshold is 10MB and the table is five orders of magnitude smaller. Explicit broadcast() hints are valuable when (a) you want to force a broadcast above the threshold, or (b) you want to be defensive against statistics being wrong. Neither applies here. The hint is harmless but adds noise; in a code review, a 'this hint is redundant — autoBroadcast handles it' comment is appropriate. Note: option C is wrong because for a 6-row table broadcast is unambiguously the fastest plan, not slower than shuffle hash join.

fields.label: Q2 — Line 41 calls .repartition("date") right before the partitioned write on line 43. Why is this here, and is it the right call at this scale?
options: Wrong — repartition before partitioned write duplicates files
Correct — repartition('date') keeps one writer per partition
Unnecessary — partitionBy already handles file layout
Wrong — the shuffle defeats AQE coalescing
fields.correctAnswer: Correct — repartition('date') keeps one writer per partition
fields.rationale: This is the correct pattern for partitioned writes at scale. partitionBy on the DataFrameWriter controls the output directory layout, but it does not control how many tasks (and therefore how many files) write to each partition. Without .repartition('date'), the previous shuffle stage's partitions (default 200, or fewer after AQE coalesce) each open their own writer for every date they contain — easily hundreds of small files per date. .repartition('date') co-locates rows of the same date on a single task before writing, so each date directory gets exactly one file. This is one of the most common production-pipeline patterns and worth recognizing on sight.

fields.label: Q3 — AQE coalescePartitions is enabled (line 8). Where in this pipeline does it actually take effect?
options: On the broadcast join (line 25)
On the groupBy aggregation (line 33).
Only on the final write
It does not take effect anywhere in this pipeline because adaptive.skewJoin.enabled was not set
fields.correctAnswer: On the groupBy aggregation (line 33).
fields.rationale: AQE coalescePartitions runs after each shuffle. The pipeline has two shuffles: the groupBy on line 33 (shuffle by substation_id, name, region, date) and the window on line 38 (shuffle by substation_id). AQE looks at the actual post-shuffle partition sizes and merges small ones together — at 12M rows partitioned by ~thousands of substations, many partitions will be small enough to merge. The broadcast join has no shuffle, so AQE coalesce cannot apply there. The file read is not a shuffle either. (Option D is wrong: skewJoin and coalescePartitions are independent AQE features.)

fields.label: Q4 — The window on line 37 uses rangeBetween(-6 * 86400, 0) on date.cast('long'). What does this guarantee about the rolling average that rowsBetween(-6, 0) would NOT?
options: Nothing — rangeBetween offset is just 6 days
rangeBetween treats -6 × 86400 as 6 calendar days back
rangeBetween includes current row, rowsBetween does not
rangeBetween is faster via sort-based optimization
fields.correctAnswer: rangeBetween treats -6 × 86400 as 6 calendar days back
fields.rationale: rangeBetween defines the frame in terms of the ORDER BY column's values, not row positions. rangeBetween(-6*86400, 0) on date-as-long means 'all rows whose date is between today-6days and today, inclusive'. rowsBetween(-6, 0) means 'the previous 6 rows plus the current row'. If a substation has missing dates (no readings on weekends, or sensor gaps), rowsBetween would walk back 6 actual rows, which could span 8, 10, or more calendar days — silently wrong. For 'last 7 calendar days', rangeBetween is the only correct choice. This is the production version of JAI5.1.

tasks.psiReference: PSI1 lesson 4 (Broadcast Joins), PSI3 lesson 2 (AQE Coalesce), PSI3 lesson 5 (Partitioned Writes); also references JAI5.1 (range vs rows)

tasks.title: Salting That Did Not Work

description.context: You are reviewing a fellow engineer's pull request for the customer-type consumption aggregation. NordGrid Energy customers are heavily skewed by type: ~60% RESIDENTIAL, ~25% COMMERCIAL, ~12% INDUSTRIAL, ~3% MUNICIPAL. The previous version of this job ran in 8 minutes; the engineer rewrote it to use 'salting' to handle the skew, and the new version runs in 11 minutes. They are asking what they did wrong. Read the pipeline and the engineer's notes, then answer the questions.
description.task: Read the pipeline carefully — line numbers are shown — then answer all the questions below.

evidence.label: snippet.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, sum, avg, lit, rand, floor, broadcast

spark = (SparkSession.builder
    .appName("customer_type_aggregation")
    .config("spark.sql.adaptive.enabled", "true")
    .config("spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled", "true")
    .config("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "200")
    .getOrCreate())

# 12M readings, heavily skewed on customer_type
meters = spark.read.parquet("silver/meters_enriched/")

# Substations: 6 rows
substations = spark.read.parquet("silver/substations/")

# === Engineer's salting attempt ===
# Step 1: add a salt column to spread the skewed key across N buckets
N_SALT = 16
meters_salted = meters.withColumn("salt", floor(rand() * N_SALT))

# Step 2: join enriched meters with substations
enriched = meters_salted.join(
    broadcast(substations.select("substation_id", "region")),
    on="substation_id",
    how="inner"
)

# Step 3: first-stage aggregation on (customer_type, salt)
stage1 = (enriched
    .groupBy("customer_type", "salt")
    .agg(
        sum("daily_kw").alias("partial_sum_kw"),
        sum(lit(1)).alias("partial_count")
    ))

# Step 4: second-stage aggregation, dropping salt
final = (stage1
    .groupBy("customer_type")
    .agg(
        sum("partial_sum_kw").alias("total_kw"),
        sum("partial_count").alias("reading_count"),
        (sum("partial_sum_kw") / sum("partial_count")).alias("avg_kw")
    ))

final.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("silver/customer_type_totals/")

fields.label: Q1 — The engineer added salting (line 21) hoping to fix the customer_type skew. The job got slower, not faster. What is the core reason salting did not help here?
options: N_SALT=16 is too small; should match shuffle.partitions
Salting fixes skewed JOINS, not groupBy aggregations
rand() is non-deterministic across stages
skewJoin.enabled disables manual salting silently
fields.correctAnswer: Salting fixes skewed JOINS, not groupBy aggregations
fields.rationale: This is the most important production lesson in the mid-level track: salting fixes skewed JOINS, not skewed pure-aggregation groupBys. For sum/count/avg, Spark's combiner (partial aggregation) runs map-side: every executor reduces its local 1.5M rows to 4 rows (one per customer_type) before shuffle. The shuffle is tiny — 4 customer_types times num_executors rows total — and skew is irrelevant because there is almost nothing to shuffle. Adding a salt column just creates a wider shuffle key (4 * 16 = 64 shuffle keys instead of 4) doing the same work, plus an extra second-stage aggregation to merge salts. The engineer applied a join-skew remedy to an aggregation problem. The core diagnostic question 'does my aggregation function support partial aggregation?' answers whether salting is worth even considering.

fields.label: Q2 — AQE skewJoin is enabled (line 7). Why doesn't AQE help with the customer_type skew either?
options: AQE skewJoin handles only SortMergeJoin skew, not groupBy
AQE skewJoin requires both sides above 256MB
AQE skewJoin is disabled when broadcast joins exist
AQE skewJoin is a Spark 4.0+ feature
fields.correctAnswer: AQE skewJoin handles only SortMergeJoin skew, not groupBy
fields.rationale: AQE skewJoin only operates on SortMergeJoins. It detects skewed partitions on the join probe side and splits them into sub-partitions. It has no equivalent for groupBy aggregations because, as covered in Q1, partial aggregation already handles skew at the map side for combinable aggregates. There is no 'AQE skew aggregate' feature because the optimization layer below it (partial aggregation) already does the right thing. The explicit name 'skewJoin' in the config is a hint.

fields.label: Q3 — What single line change would actually make this pipeline faster than the original 8-minute version?
options: Increase N_SALT from 16 to 256
Remove the salting entirely.
Replace the broadcast() hint on line 23 with a SortMergeJoin
Change shuffle.partitions from 200 to 16 to match N_SALT
fields.correctAnswer: Remove the salting entirely.
fields.rationale: The original 8-minute pipeline was already correct. The 'fix' added overhead. Going back to plain .groupBy('customer_type').agg(...) is the right call — partial aggregation handles the skew at the map side, and the final shuffle volume is essentially zero (4 customer_types * num_executors rows). The salting added a wider shuffle key, an extra aggregation stage, and the cost of computing rand() on 12M rows, all for no benefit. This is a textbook case of over-engineering: the engineer reached for a remedy that addresses a different problem (join skew) than they have (aggregation on a skewed key, which is not actually a problem).

fields.label: Q4 — Which kind of aggregation WOULD have benefited from salting?
options: Any aggregation on a skewed key benefits from salting
Aggregations using collect_list or percentile_approx
approx_count_distinct over a high-cardinality key
None — partial aggregation already handles all skew
fields.correctAnswer: Aggregations using collect_list or percentile_approx
fields.rationale: Salting in aggregation is worth it ONLY when partial aggregation is disabled — which happens when the aggregation function cannot be expressed as a mergeable buffer. collect_list and collect_set are the canonical examples (the buffer is a list, and merging is just concatenation, which is technically combinable but Spark doesn't enable a partial stage for collection aggregators in the same way). Custom UDAFs that don't override merge correctly also fall here (echo of JAI3.3 / JAI3.4). For those, the entire data volume per group hits the shuffle, and a salting + two-stage aggregation pattern can split the work — provided you can write the merge step. For sum/count/min/max/avg/stddev, salting is unambiguously a code smell.

tasks.psiReference: PSI1 lesson 6 (Skew); PSI3 lesson 1 (AQE skewJoin); also references JAI2.3 (skew in plan), JAI3.3 / JAI3.4 (combiner behavior)

tasks.title: Reading the Plan: Three-Way Join with Aggregation

description.context: You are reviewing a pipeline that joins three tables (meters, substations, tariffs) and aggregates per region per day. The author has included the output of df.explain(mode='extended') in their PR. Your reviewer asks you to read both the code AND the physical plan and answer the questions below. Cluster: AQE enabled, autoBroadcastJoinThreshold=10MB, default shuffle.partitions=200.
description.task: Read the pipeline carefully — line numbers are shown — then answer all the questions below.

evidence.label: snippet.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, sum, avg, count

spark = (SparkSession.builder
    .appName("regional_billing")
    .config("spark.sql.adaptive.enabled", "true")
    .config("spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled", "true")
    .config("spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold", "10485760")
    .getOrCreate())

# 12M readings
meters = spark.read.parquet("silver/meters_enriched/")
# 6 substations
substations = spark.read.parquet("silver/substations/")
# 4 tariffs
tariffs = spark.read.parquet("silver/tariffs/")
# 800K customer accounts
customers = spark.read.parquet("silver/customers/")

result = (meters
    .join(substations.select("substation_id", "region"), on="substation_id", how="inner")
    .join(tariffs.select("tariff_code", "price_eur_per_kwh"), on="tariff_code", how="inner")
    .join(customers.select("customer_id", "customer_type"), on="customer_id", how="inner")
    .groupBy("region", "customer_type", "date")
    .agg(
        sum("daily_kw").alias("total_kw"),
        avg("price_eur_per_kwh").alias("avg_price"),
        count("*").alias("reading_count")
    )
)

result.explain(mode="extended")
result.write.mode("overwrite").partitionBy("date").parquet("silver/regional_billing/")

fields.label: Q1 — How many shuffles (Exchange operators) does this plan contain, and what triggers each?
options: 1 shuffle: just the final groupBy
2 shuffles: customer_id join + final groupBy
5 shuffles: each join plus the groupBy
0 shuffles — broadcasts cover all joins
fields.correctAnswer: 2 shuffles: customer_id join + final groupBy
fields.rationale: The plan has three Exchange (hashpartitioning) operators: one each for the two sides of the customer SortMergeJoin (lines with `Exchange hashpartitioning(customer_id, 200)`), and one for the final groupBy (`Exchange hashpartitioning(region, customer_type, date, 200)`). The two BroadcastHashJoins use BroadcastExchange — that is a broadcast (one-to-many copy of a small table) NOT a shuffle (all-to-all redistribution). Counting shuffles by counting Exchange-with-hashpartitioning is the right rule. Note the plan is read bottom-up: file scans first, then the broadcast joins build on top of the meters scan, then the SortMergeJoin shuffles, then the partial aggregate, then the final shuffle, then the final aggregate.

fields.label: Q2 — The substations table (6 rows) and tariffs table (4 rows) are joined as BroadcastHashJoin. The customers table (800K rows) is joined as SortMergeJoin. Why?
options: Because the customers table comes after tariffs in the join order
800K rows of customer data exceeds the autoBroadcastJoinThreshold of 10MB.
Because customer_id is a string and the others are integers
Because BroadcastHashJoin only supports up to 2 broadcasts per query
fields.correctAnswer: 800K rows of customer data exceeds the autoBroadcastJoinThreshold of 10MB.
fields.rationale: Catalyst chooses BroadcastHashJoin when one side fits in driver memory and below autoBroadcastJoinThreshold (10MB here). Tiny reference tables (substations 6 rows, tariffs 4 rows) are obvious broadcasts. The customers table at 800K rows and ~80MB is well over the threshold — broadcasting it would risk OOM on the driver and on every executor (echo of JAI2.4 — broadcast hint OOM). SortMergeJoin shuffles both sides by the join key, sorts each partition, and merges — slower than broadcast but the only viable plan when neither side fits. The fact that the plan picks SMJ here is exactly correct given the data sizes.

fields.label: Q3 — The plan shows TWO HashAggregate operators: one with `partial_sum`, `partial_avg`, `partial_count`, and one with `sum`, `avg`, `count`. What does this two-stage structure tell you?
options: It's a bug — the second aggregate is redundant
Partial aggregation is enabled and working correctly
avg() runs twice and the results may differ
Planner optimization that only applies without AQE
fields.correctAnswer: Partial aggregation is enabled and working correctly
fields.rationale: The two-stage HashAggregate structure (partial_xxx feeding into final xxx) is the partial aggregation pattern. It works because sum/count/avg can be expressed as a mergeable buffer: sum-of-partials = sum-of-totals, count-of-partials = count-of-totals, and avg's partial buffer is (sum, count) which merges trivially. Spark runs the partial stage map-side on each task before the shuffle, which is what makes pure-aggregation skew (like the customer_type 60% RESIDENTIAL skew from JAI2.3) a non-issue. If you saw `ObjectHashAggregate` with no `partial_xxx` rows, that would tell you partial aggregation is disabled — and you should worry about shuffle volume. (Echo of JAI3.3.)

fields.label: Q4 — The plan shows `AQEShuffleRead coalesced` above the final groupBy's shuffle. What does this mean concretely?
options: AQE merged small post-shuffle partitions into larger ones
Shuffle data was lost and AQE recomputed it
The plan is final and won't change at runtime
Skew was detected and the partition was split
fields.correctAnswer: AQE merged small post-shuffle partitions into larger ones
fields.rationale: AQEShuffleRead with `coalesced` annotation means AQE looked at the actual byte sizes of the 200 post-shuffle partitions and merged adjacent small ones together to reach the advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes target (default 64MB). After partial aggregation, the data volume going into the final groupBy shuffle is small (a few rows per region/customer_type/date per task), so most of the 200 partitions are tiny — AQE merges them down to a handful, dramatically reducing task overhead. The number of partitions chosen is visible in the runtime plan after the stage completes. (Echo of JAI4.1.)

tasks.psiReference: PSI1 lessons 4-6 (Joins and Plans), PSI3 lessons 1-3 (AQE); also references JAI2.4 (broadcast OOM), JAI3.2 (partial+final), JAI4.1 (AQE coalesce)

tasks.title: Closing Exercise: Five Mid-Level Bugs

description.context: Final exercise of the mid-level track. You are reviewing a 'production-ready' pipeline for monthly customer billing at NordGrid Energy. The author has been at the company for three months and is proud of this PR. The pipeline runs in 47 minutes on the production cluster (the senior version of the same job runs in 6 minutes). The author asks you to review before they ask their team lead. There are FIVE distinct mid-level mistakes in this code, each one drawn from a different module of this track. Find them all.
description.task: Read the pipeline carefully — line numbers are shown — then answer all the questions below.

evidence.label: snippet.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import (
    col, sum, avg, count, lit, broadcast, udf, collect_list, max as spark_max
)
from pyspark.sql.types import DoubleType
from pyspark.sql.window import Window

spark = (SparkSession.builder
    .appName("monthly_customer_billing")
    .config("spark.sql.adaptive.enabled", "true")
    .getOrCreate())

# 12M readings for the month
meters = spark.read.parquet("silver/meters_enriched/")
# 800K customers (~80MB)
customers = spark.read.parquet("silver/customers/")
# 4 tariffs
tariffs = spark.read.parquet("silver/tariffs/")

# === Bug 1 region ===
# Get the latest reading per meter (for end-of-month meter check)
w_latest = Window.partitionBy("meter_id")
meters_with_latest = meters.withColumn("latest_reading", spark_max("daily_kw").over(w_latest))
meters_at_latest = meters_with_latest.filter(col("daily_kw") == col("latest_reading"))

# === Bug 2 region ===
# Enrich with customer info — broadcast since 'customers is small'
enriched = meters.join(
    broadcast(customers.select("customer_id", "customer_type", "name")),
    on="customer_id",
    how="inner"
)
# tariffs is also joined
enriched = enriched.join(tariffs.select("tariff_code", "price_eur_per_kwh"), on="tariff_code", how="inner")

# === Bug 3 region ===
# Compute weighted average price per customer using a UDF over collected list
# 'because that's how I'd do it in Python'
@udf(returnType=DoubleType())
def weighted_avg(kw_list, prices):
    if not kw_list:
        return 0.0
    total = sum(k * p for k, p in zip(kw_list, prices))
    weight = sum(kw_list)
    return float(total / weight) if weight else 0.0

per_customer_kw = (enriched
    .groupBy("customer_id", "customer_type")
    .agg(
        collect_list("daily_kw").alias("kw_list"),
        collect_list("price_eur_per_kwh").alias("price_list"),
        sum("daily_kw").alias("total_kw")
    )
    .withColumn("weighted_price", weighted_avg(col("kw_list"), col("price_list")))
    .drop("kw_list", "price_list"))

# === Bug 4 region ===
# Aggregate per customer_type — known skew: 60% RESIDENTIAL, 25% COMMERCIAL,
# 12% INDUSTRIAL, 3% MUNICIPAL. Author assumes AQE will handle it.
by_type = (per_customer_kw
    .groupBy("customer_type")
    .agg(
        sum("total_kw").alias("type_total_kw"),
        avg("weighted_price").alias("type_avg_price"),
        count("*").alias("customer_count")
    ))

# === Bug 5 region ===
# Get top customer per type for the leaderboard. Author chose self-join because
# they 'find window functions confusing'.
max_per_type = (per_customer_kw
    .groupBy("customer_type")
    .agg(spark_max("total_kw").alias("max_kw")))
top_customers = (per_customer_kw
    .join(max_per_type, on="customer_type", how="inner")
    .filter(col("total_kw") == col("max_kw"))
    .select("customer_type", "customer_id", "total_kw"))

by_type.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("silver/billing/by_type/")
top_customers.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("silver/billing/top_customers/")

fields.label: Q1 — Bug 1 region (lines 22-24): Window.partitionBy('meter_id') with spark_max('daily_kw') and no orderBy. Then a filter where daily_kw == latest_reading. What is wrong?
options: Nothing — Window over partitionBy is fine here
max('daily_kw') returns the max value, not the latest row
Should use max(struct('date','daily_kw')) for the latest row
Window.partitionBy without orderBy is illegal in Spark 3.5
fields.correctAnswer: max('daily_kw') returns the max value, not the latest row
fields.rationale: Two compounding bugs in three lines. First, max('daily_kw') returns the highest CONSUMPTION value per meter, not the most recent reading — these are completely different things. The author confused 'latest in time' with 'maximum in value'. Second, the window has no orderBy, so even if max were the right function, partition-without-order has the cumulative-sum-without-order semantic from JAI1.3. The correct pattern: row_number().over(Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy(col('date').desc())) and filter where row_number==1. Note also: this whole computed column meters_at_latest is never used downstream — dead code that costs a window shuffle for nothing.

fields.label: Q2 — Bug 2 region (lines 27-32): broadcast(customers.select(...)) on an 800K row, ~80MB table. What goes wrong at production scale?
options: Nothing — 80MB is below the broadcast threshold
broadcast() collects 800K rows to the driver and OOMs
select() before broadcast() prevents the hint from working
broadcast() requires the build side partitioned by the key
fields.correctAnswer: broadcast() collects 800K rows to the driver and OOMs
fields.rationale: broadcast() forces a broadcast regardless of size. With 800K customers at ~100 bytes/row plus HashedRelation overhead (typically 2-4× on-disk size), the broadcast can easily exceed 200MB. That hits the driver during collection (driver OOM risk — exactly JAI2.4) and every executor's broadcast variable storage. autoBroadcastJoinThreshold is 10MB by default precisely because broadcasts above that risk OOM. The fix is to delete the broadcast() hint — Catalyst's SortMergeJoin is the correct plan for an 80MB table joined to 12M rows.

fields.label: Q3 — Bug 3 region (lines 39-55): collect_list of daily_kw and prices into Python lists, then a Python UDF computes weighted average. What is the cost?
options: It is fine — collect_list scales with the partial aggregator
collect_list disables partial aggregation; full lists shuffle
Python lists use O(n²) memory for the UDF
UDF returns DoubleType but should return DecimalType
fields.correctAnswer: collect_list disables partial aggregation; full lists shuffle
fields.rationale: This is the most expensive bug in the pipeline. collect_list disables partial aggregation: the entire (kw, price) pair must be shuffled to one task per customer (echo of JAI3.4). Then a Python UDF runs on every collected list, paying serialization cost (Arrow if Python UDF is vectorized, pickle if not) for each customer. The correct rewrite is a one-liner using Spark expressions: per_customer_kw = enriched.groupBy('customer_id', 'customer_type').agg(sum('daily_kw').alias('total_kw'), (sum(col('daily_kw') * col('price_eur_per_kwh')) / sum('daily_kw')).alias('weighted_price')). Pure Spark expressions, partial aggregation enabled, no UDF, no collect_list. This single change probably cuts the job from 47 minutes to under 10.

fields.label: Q4 — Bug 4 region (lines 60-67): groupBy('customer_type') on data known to be 60/25/12/3 skewed. The author assumes AQE handles it. Does it?
options: Yes — AQE skewJoin handles any skewed key
Yes — but only when partial aggregation is disabled
No — AQE skewJoin only handles SortMergeJoin skew
No — AQE is disabled by default in production
fields.correctAnswer: No — AQE skewJoin only handles SortMergeJoin skew
fields.rationale: AQE skewJoin only handles SortMergeJoin probe-side skew. There is no AQE feature for aggregation skew because the underlying optimization (partial aggregation) handles it natively for combinable functions like sum/count/avg/min/max — the per-task partial aggregation reduces each task's data to one row per (customer_type) BEFORE the shuffle, so the shuffle volume is tiny regardless of skew. The author got the right answer (don't worry about it) for the wrong reason (assumed AQE would step in). The actual problem hiding here is that bug 3's collect_list broke partial aggregation in the upstream stage; this stage's input is bloated. (Echoes JAI2.3 and JAI3.3.)

fields.label: Q5 — Bug 5 region (lines 70-78): self-join to get 'top customer per type' instead of using a window function. What is the cost difference, and is there another bug?
options: Self-join is faster than a window for top-N queries
Self-join causes a second full shuffle of per_customer_kw
Both approaches are equivalent at this scale
Self-join requires broadcast() on max_per_type
fields.correctAnswer: Self-join causes a second full shuffle of per_customer_kw
fields.rationale: Two issues. Cost: self-join requires a second full shuffle (group by customer_type to get max, then broadcast or shuffle-join back to per_customer_kw). The window-function version (row_number partition by customer_type order by total_kw desc, then filter where row_number==1) needs ONE shuffle. At 800K rows of per_customer_kw, the difference is meaningful but not catastrophic. Correctness: the filter `total_kw == max_kw` returns ALL customers tied for the max (echo of JAI1.2). For a leaderboard 'top customer per type', that means RESIDENTIAL might return 5 customers if 5 are tied at the max. row_number() guarantees exactly one row per type — but uses a deterministic tiebreak you can control. dense_rank() returns all ties. rank() returns all ties with gaps. The author's choice of self-join+filter has the worst combination: slower AND ambiguous on ties.
